title: SDK keepalive misuse triggering proxy handshake storms and graceful reject shim
preview:
Summary:
Customers reported recurring 5xx waves and long tail latency spikes during high-volume websocket-to-rest fallback traffic. We traced root cause to abusive SDK keepalive/short-lived connection patterns that trigger repeated TLS renegotiation at our edge proxies. Those renegotiations exhaust proxy worker threads, causing queued requests, connection resets, and client retries which amplify the failure into tenant-visible 502/504 waves.

Why this is unique: Unlike previous incidents that were primarily backpressure or KV cache related, this issue is a connection-management pathology driven by (a) an SDK pattern that reopened connections in <2s cycles and (b) our proxy TLS session handling which did a heavy handshake path per reconnection. The combination produces a handshake storm during tenant surges and a correlated retry amplification across the hosted API surface.

Scope & Impact:
- Affected surface: hosted-api edge proxy and short-lived REST fallback flows for streaming SDKs; some Dedicated customers who route through our proxy mesh saw similar symptoms when using older SDK versions.
- Symptoms: intermittent 5xx (502/504), long request queuing (p50->p99 increased by 3-10x), high proxy CPU and handshake queue length spikes, and increased customer retries visible in request logs.
- Customer escalations: 3 enterprise customers escalated with production outages during peak hours; engineering received multiple P1/R1 alerts.

Investigation notes / evidence:
- Reproducer: internal load harness that opens 2000 ephemeral connections/second with 1–3s keepalive interval reproduces the handshake queue and proxy worker saturation in staging (staged-repro-2026-03-09).
- Traces: edge-proxy spans show TLS handshake timeouts correlating with socket accept latencies; per-tenant queues show spike at tracer tag tenant_id: cust-42.
- Logs: TLS session cache miss rates increased 8x during events; connection counts per worker exceeded soft threshold and then worker threads blocked on OpenSSL renegotiation.
- Timeline: 2026-03-08 first customer ticket; 2026-03-09 confirmed reproducible; 2026-03-10 emergency patch designed; 2026-03-12 canary rollout; 2026-03-14 escalation closed for two customers after mitigation.

Proposed fixes (short and medium term):
1) Hotfix (short): graceful-reject shim in edge-proxy: if per-worker connection-accept backlog > 75% and TLS-handshake-rate > threshold, start returning 429/503 (configurable) with Retry-After and idempotency guidance for REST fallbacks. This prevents handshake pileup and stops client retries from amplifying the surge.
2) Connection-pool hardening (medium): implement shared TLS session cache accessible across proxy workers (reduce full-handshakes), plus faster handshake offload path for ephemeral connections.
3) SDK guidance + patch (medium): publish new SDK release that changes default keepalive/backoff behavior, batch fallback reconnects, and emits an opt-in connection reuse mode. Coordinate with SDK and DX teams for release notes and migration guide.
4) Long-term (Q2): telemetry-driven per-tenant admission control: dynamically cap ephemeral connection opens and switch high-churn tenants to a streaming-first fallback or reserved capacity.

Action plan / roll forward steps:
- Hotfix PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/6712 (edge-proxy: reject-shim + metrics). Target CI green and small canary on staging cluster A on 2026-03-11.
- Canary: run for 48h on <1% traffic then expand to 10% if error budget healthy. Observability checks: handshake-rate, worker-accept-backlog, 5xx-count, customer-surface p99 latency.
- SDK release: coordinate with DX for v1.4.7 (default keepalive interval increase from 2s->60s, enable connection reuse). Draft release notes: drive.google.com/drive/sdk-keepalive-migration (internal draft).
- Customer comms: Support/CS to send targeted guidance to impacted customers with immediate mitigation steps (disable aggressive fallback, upgrade SDK). Ticket templates prepared at confluence.redwood.com/esc/962830.

Testing & verification:
- Re-run the staged repro harness to confirm handshake-rate drops by >80% with hotfix enabled.
- Load test across multiple tenants to ensure no cross-tenant bleed.
- Chaos test: simulate large numbers of ephemeral reconnects while monitoring proxy worker saturation and overarching p99 latency variance.

Runbook updates:
- Add a checklist on detecting handshake storms (metric thresholds + queries) and immediate throttling steps: enable reject-shim, scale proxy worker pool, notify customers. Runbook PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runbooks/pull/88.

Open questions / trade-offs:
- Returning synthetic 429/503 reduces customer-received 5xx but may break strict SLA customers. We need to gate behavior per-tenant tier (enterprise should get soft-fail with longer Retry-After or routed to Dedicated).
- Shared TLS session cache complexity vs operational risk of cross-worker state. Estimate: 3-4 week effort, requires careful security review.

Post-mitigation followups:
- Postmortem + RCA to be published by 2026-03-25. Draft notes will include timeline, mitigations, and SDK changes.
- Customer-specific remediation: offer Dedicated pilot or reserved connection pool to impacted enterprise customers if they cannot migrate SDK behavior rapidly.

title: SDK quickstart & localized sample catalog for streaming + function-calling + structured output
preview:
Goal: deliver a curated quickstart and localized sample catalog demonstrating streaming responses, function/tool calling, and structured output across the SDKs (Python, Node, Go). The kit should include: (1) three minimal quickstarts (streaming chat with function calls, structured JSON responses for form extraction, and streaming embeddings pipeline), (2) localized README translations for en, fr, de, ja, and (3) a sample verification CI that runs smoke tests and validates structured schema outputs. This ticket coordinates PM, Docs, SDK Eng, Design, and Localization to produce publish-ready artifacts and release notes entries.

Scope: author sample code, write short HOWTOs for each platform, create a release-notes matrix tying sample files to feature flags and API changes, design lightweight diagrams for console docs, onboard translations, and add telemetry checks to telemetry-dashboard for sample usage.

Out of scope: full tutorial videos, long-form blog posts (those are separate marketing tasks).

title: Roadmap Orchestra playbook for cross-team dependency weeks
preview: Create an operational playbook (the 'Orchestra') to run recurring 1-week dependency windows around major roadmap changes. This ticket captures the scope, roles, comms, and checklist for two objectives: 1) reduce cross-team unblock time during critical rollouts, and 2) produce an auditable handoff for Exec/Board readouts and post-rollout retros. The playbook standardizes pre-window validation, live coordination, and post-window regression checks so that PM, Eng, and Design have a shared cadence for complex initiative merges and infra migrations.

title: Client adapter contract: resumable iterators, idempotent paging, and streaming backpressure
preview: Define a single, language-idiomatic contract and acceptance criteria for paginated fetches, resumable iterators, retry semantics, and streaming APIs across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. The goal is to enable zero-downtime client upgrades and predictable developer expectations when switching runtime models or server fallbacks.

The goal is to enable zero-downtime client upgrades and predictable developer expectations when switching runtime models or server fallbacks.

title: Adaptive toggle evolution & client migration kit for streaming/tool-call structured outputs
preview:
Purpose: Provide a prescriptive migration kit and operational play for rolling behavioral changes that affect streaming, tool/function calling, and structured outputs. The kit must minimize customer breakage, accelerate client SDK migrations, and provide reproducible backcompat tests that exercise streaming+toolcall edge-cases.

Context: We are introducing a set of behavior flag changes to standardize structured-output schema negotiation and to fix long-standing streaming termination edge cases that cause downstream tool-call parsers to mis-interpret partial chunks. These changes improve correctness and long-tail cost, but risk client regressions in older SDKs and bespoke tool integrations. This ticket is the PM-facing orchestration: docs, migration guide, staged deprecation timeline, success signals, and the required acceptance criteria for engineering to implement the hooks and tests.

Goals:
- Define naming and lifetime for the behavior flags and an opt-in/opt-out progression plan.
- Produce a client migration kit (checklist + SDK snippets + example server-side fallback policies).
- Specify a backcompat test matrix and telemetry signals for automated gating (canary -> gradual ramp -> retire).
- Draft runbook for incidents caused by flag toggles and automated rollback triggers.

Out of scope: Implementing the runtime kernel changes themselves (tracked in ENG tickets), low-level quantization recommendations, or non-API surface UX changes to Console dashboards (though telemetry hooks should be available).

title: Inkwell ingest gateway and schema fallback coordinator
preview: Goal: Build 'Inkwell', a resilient ingest gateway that performs probabilistic schema scoring, lineage annotation, and coordinates an automatic schema-fallback path to preserve customers' console analytics when upstream producers are noisy or schema-evolving. Scope: (1) a streaming pre-processor that scores events against the active contract using a lightweight probabilistic scorer (fast decision path) and tags events with a schema_confidence score; (2) a small nearline repair pipeline (repair.shim) that attempts minimal coercions for low-confidence events and emits repaired copies to the main pipeline; (3) a fallback coordinator which, under sustained contract drift, routes events to a permissive "compat" topic and surfaces a summarized mutation batch to the contract registry for review; (4) operator-facing metrics, traces, and a canary rollout plan. Acceptance criteria: (A) end-to-end ingest latency increase <= 6ms p95 for median event size under normal load; (B) high-confidence pass-through rate >= 95% for stable producers; (C) repaired event accuracy >= 92% on synthetic drift suite; (D) automatic fallback triggers are auditable in Console and reversible via rollout; (E) alerts fire for fallback rate > 0.5% sustained over 10m. Design highlights and trade-offs: - Probabilistic scoring vs full schema validation: we choose a two-tier approach to keep fast-path latency low while still catching regressions. The scorer uses feature hashes (field presence, type fingerprints, enum hits) and a trained logistic regression returned as protobuf for edge evaluation. - Nearline repair: only safe coercions (string->enum mapping via whitelist, number coercions with bounds) are attempted; more invasive repairs are quarantined for human review. - Backpressure: integrate with existing ingest token-bucket and add a soft-shed policy that prioritizes high-confidence events for immediate processing, while buffering low-confidence events to the repair queue. - Observability: add a new per-customer metric series ingest.schema_confidence.p{50,95,99}, fallback.rate, repair.success.rate, and tracing spans for scorer->repair->router. - Security: repair module runs with least privilege and does not send raw payloads to third parties; KMS access for sensitive fields is proxied via platform key service. Migration and rollout plan: 1) Deploy Inkwell scorer behind feature flag for 5% of traffic in staging; 2) Run in 'observe-only' mode for 72h and capture metrics + false-positive/false-negative counts against golden dataset; 3) Enable nearline repairs for canary customers with explicit opt-in; 4) Expand to 25% then 100% with rollback window. Monitoring and runbook: create a Console dashboard with top failing producers, schema_confidence histograms by producer, and a runbook for fallback investigations and manual re-ingestion. Testing: unit tests for scorer determinism, e2e tests with Synthetic Drift Suite, chaos testing to validate fallback under transient Kafka partitions. Open questions: - How to version scorer models across tenants? (per-tenant vs global) proposal: global model with tenant-specific feature biases recorded in metadata. - Threshold tuning strategy: do we allow auto-tune based on rolling false-positive rate or require manual ops approval? Implementation plan: Phase 1 (3 sprints) - scorer library + integration with ingest proxy, metrics hooks. Phase 2 (2 sprints) - nearline repair pipeline and repair APIs. Phase 3 (2 sprints) - fallback coordinator, console UI surfaces, and rollout automation. Backward compatibility: preserve the current ingest schema contract and ensure compat topic subscribers can opt into permissive consumption. Long-term roadmap: couple Inkwell signals into Optimize and Console's Quality Monitoring to trigger model/quantization advice when certain traffic patterns correlate with degraded inference quality.

title: Real-world client-mix streaming harness with paced tokenflow and p95 regression gate
preview:
Goal: build a load-testing harness that emulates a realistic mix of streaming chat clients (SSE + WebSocket) with heterogeneous token pacing, intermittent connection churn, and per-tenant KV-cache warm/cold states. The tool should focus on producing stable tokens/sec curves, p95 end-to-end latency tracking for first-byte and last-byte, and automated regression detection with signal decomposition (cold-cache vs. warm-cache vs. network variance).

Why: Existing harnesses produce high-level tokenflow numbers but fail to reproduce real-world client diversity (mobile vs. desktop, slow vs. aggressive token consumption, backgrounded clients that resume). This causes missed regressions where an optimization improves median throughput but regresses p95 for low-throughput long-tail clients.

Scope: implement a harness and analysis pipeline that can: (1) generate client mixes with configurable distributions of session length, message size, inter-token pacing, and reconnection patterns; (2) run at cluster scale (100k simulated clients) across multiple runner nodes; (3) label traces by client archetype and cache state and emit aggregated metrics (tokens/sec, p95 first-byte, p95 final-token, error-rate) per archetype and overall; (4) integrate with regression-detector to automatically create alerts and a rollback BZ when p95 increases beyond configurable thresholds.

Non-goals: provide a production traffic generator for customer traffic validation (this is for infra/regression detection only), or implement protocol-level application logic beyond what’s necessary to emulate token pacing and connection fidelity.

title: Dynamic runbook injection and PagerDuty context enrichment for traced incidents
preview: Problem: When high-severity incidents fire, responders spend time gathering context (top traces, correlated logs/metrics, relevant dashboards) and manually selecting runbooks. This increases MTTR and causes inconsistent postmortems. Goal: Implement a system that automatically enriches PagerDuty incidents and Slack alert threads with curated incident context (trace links, top correlated metrics/log snippets), and injects a suggested, editable runbook into the incident and Slack thread. Additionally seed a draft postmortem with an initial timeline generated from traces and critical logs to accelerate RCA.

title: Eviction-aware striped KV allocation for asymmetric tensor/pipeline shards
preview:
Background: In multi-GPU serving with tensor and pipeline parallelism, KV cache memory pressure is highly asymmetric: some pipeline stages / tensor shards accumulate large KV segments for long contexts while others remain underutilized. Continuous batching exacerbates this because request sequence-length skew and batching windows change per-step, leading to transient OOMs or suboptimal conservative reservations.

Goal: Design and implement an eviction-aware striped allocation strategy for the KV cache that: 1) shards KV storage into fine-grained "stripes" spread across GPUs, 2) supports local eviction/compaction policies to reclaim memory without global pauses, 3) selectively replicates small hot entries to reduce cross-shard RPCs, and 4) integrates with the serving runtime's continuous batching and routing logic to minimize tail latency and avoid OOMs.

Approach overview:
- Stripe layout: split a model's KV address space into small fixed-size stripes (configurable, e.g., 4–16 MB). Stripes are assigned to GPUs using a placement function that considers current free memory, tensor-parallel mapping, and pipeline stage locality. This produces a striped distribution rather than large contiguous slab shards.
- Localized eviction: each GPU manages an LRU-ish eviction within its stripes. When memory pressure rises, local compaction (prefix trimming / slot coalescing) and selective eviction occur at stripe granularity to avoid global coordination.
- Hot-entry micro-replication: small, frequently-accessed KV entries (identified by access counters) can be replicated to neighboring GPUs to serve repeated lookups without RPC. Replication is limited by size and age thresholds to bound memory overhead.
- Background compaction + graceful reclaim: a background worker compacts fragmented stripes during low-utilization windows observed via the runtime. Compaction is prioritized by fragmentation score and predicted future reuse (from access traces).
- Coordination with batching and routing: the scheduler exposes a stripe affinity hint per request to encourage assignment of compute for a token to a GPU holding most of its hot KV stripes. If affinity can't be met, the runtime uses optimized async fetch paths with prioritized prefetch for active batches.
- Fallback policy: when a GPU hits critical pressure, the shard either triggers remote fetch with preemptive eviction or falls back to a compatible lower-memory model variant (managed by the routing policy).

Design decisions and rationale: 
- Why stripes vs slabs: finer-grained stripes reduce the imbalance effect when some tokens require long contexts; they let us reclaim small regions without moving large contiguous blocks.
- Why local eviction: global coordination adds latency and contention with continuous batches; localized policies enable bounded-latency reclaim.
- Why selective replication: replication reduces RPCs and tail stalls for hot keys; restricting it to small entries keeps memory overhead acceptable.
- Why stripe affinity: aligning compute to data reduces cross-GPU transfers during the inner loop of token generation, improving tail latency under continuous batching.

Trade-offs: 
- Complexity vs benefit: the allocator and runtime hooks increase system complexity and testing surface. However, we expect material reductions in OOM events and tail latency on skewed workloads.
- Memory overhead: micro-replication consumes extra memory (target < 5% of total KV memory).
- Increased metadata: stripe mapping and per-entry counters require additional metadata; mitigate with compact counters and periodic decay.

Milestones: 
1) Simulation harness (2w): extend the existing KV pressure simulator to model continuous batching and tensor/pipeline topologies. Produce baseline OOM and tail-latency curves.
2) Placement & stripe allocator prototype (2w): implement stripe assignment function and in-memory allocator with local eviction and fragmentation metrics.
3) Runtime integration (3w): add stripe affinity hints to scheduler, async remote fetch path, and background compactor hooks.
4) Micro-replication engine (2w): hot-entry detection, bounded replication, and eviction awareness.
5) Canary rollout & benchmarking (2w): run synthetic and customer-like workloads, compare against baseline.
6) Full rollout & cleanup (1w): telemetry dashboards, safety thresholds, and docs.

Acceptance criteria: 
- OOM event rate under skewed continuous-batching workloads reduced by >= 80% vs current slab-based allocator in the simulator.
- 99.9th percentile token generation latency improves or remains within 5% of baseline on representative workloads.
- Memory overhead from replication < 5% of total KV usage on average.
- No regressions in single-GPU baseline (must pass unit and integration tests).

Metrics & observability: 
- Per-GPU KV memory usage and fragmented bytes per stripe.
- Stripe eviction count, compaction time, and bytes reclaimed.
- Cross-GPU fetch latency histogram and RPC counts.
- Tail token latency (p50/p95/p99) with and without affinity hits.
- OOM events and emergency fallbacks triggered.

Test plan: 
- Unit tests for allocator invariants, eviction correctness, and replication bounds.
- Integration tests with the serving runtime simulating pipeline/tensor topologies.

text:
Background: In multi-GPU serving with tensor and pipeline parallelism, KV cache memory pressure is highly asymmetric: some pipeline stages / tensor shards accumulate large KV segments for long contexts while others remain underutilized. Continuous batching exacerbates this because request sequence-length skew and batching windows change per-step, leading to transient OOMs or suboptimal conservative reservations.

Goal: Design and implement an eviction-aware striped allocation strategy for the KV cache that: 1) shards KV storage into fine-grained "stripes" spread across GPUs, 2) supports local eviction/compaction policies to reclaim memory without global pauses, 3) selectively replicates small hot entries to reduce cross-shard RPCs, and 4) integrates with the serving runtime's continuous batching and routing logic to minimize tail latency and avoid OOMs.

Approach overview:
- Stripe layout: split a model's KV address space into small fixed-size stripes (configurable, e.g., 4–16 MB). Stripes are assigned to GPUs using a placement function that considers current free memory, tensor-parallel mapping, and pipeline stage locality. This produces a striped distribution rather than large contiguous slab shards.
- Localized eviction: each GPU manages an LRU-ish eviction within its stripes. When memory pressure rises, local compaction (prefix trimming / slot coalescing) and selective eviction occur at stripe granularity to avoid global coordination.
- Hot-entry micro-replication: small, frequently-accessed KV entries (identified by access counters) can be replicated to neighboring GPUs to serve repeated lookups without RPC. Replication is limited by size and age thresholds to bound memory overhead.
- Background compaction + graceful reclaim: a background worker compacts fragmented stripes during low-utilization windows observed via the runtime. Compaction is prioritized by fragmentation score and predicted future reuse (from access traces).
- Coordination with batching and routing: the scheduler exposes a stripe affinity hint per request to encourage assignment of compute for a token to a GPU holding most of its hot KV stripes. If affinity can't be met, the runtime uses optimized async fetch paths with prioritized prefetch for active batches.
- Fallback policy: when a GPU hits critical pressure, the shard either triggers remote fetch with preemptive eviction or falls back to a compatible lower-memory model variant (managed by the routing policy).

Design decisions and rationale: 
- Why stripes vs slabs: finer-grained stripes reduce the imbalance effect when some tokens require long contexts; they let us reclaim small regions without moving large contiguous blocks.
- Why local eviction: global coordination adds latency and contention with continuous batches; localized policies enable bounded-latency reclaim.
- Why selective replication: replication reduces RPCs and tail stalls for hot keys; restricting it to small entries keeps memory overhead acceptable.
- Why stripe affinity: aligning compute to data reduces cross-GPU transfers during the inner loop of token generation, improving tail latency under continuous batching.

Trade-offs: 
- Complexity vs benefit: the allocator and runtime hooks increase system complexity and testing surface. However, we expect material reductions in OOM events and tail latency on skewed workloads.
- Memory overhead: micro-replication consumes extra memory (target < 5% of total KV memory).
- Increased metadata: stripe mapping and per-entry counters require additional metadata; mitigate with compact counters and periodic decay.

Milestones: 
1) Simulation harness (2w): extend the existing KV pressure simulator to model continuous batching and tensor/pipeline topologies. Produce baseline OOM and tail-latency curves.
2) Placement & stripe allocator prototype (2w): implement stripe assignment function and in-memory allocator with local eviction and fragmentation metrics.
3) Runtime integration (3w): add stripe affinity hints to scheduler, async remote fetch path, and background compactor hooks.
4) Micro-replication engine (2w): hot-entry detection, bounded replication, and eviction awareness.
5) Canary rollout & benchmarking (2w): run synthetic and customer-like workloads, compare against baseline.
6) Full rollout & cleanup (1w): telemetry dashboards, safety thresholds, and docs.

Acceptance criteria: 
- OOM event rate under skewed continuous-batching workloads reduced by >= 80% vs current slab-based allocator in the simulator.
- 99.9th percentile token generation latency improves or remains within 5% of baseline on representative workloads.
- Memory overhead from replication < 5% of total KV usage on average.
- No regressions in single-GPU baseline (must pass unit and integration tests).

Metrics & observability: 
- Per-GPU KV memory usage and fragmented bytes per stripe.
- Stripe eviction count, compaction time, and bytes reclaimed.
- Cross-GPU fetch latency histogram and RPC counts.
- Tail token latency (p50/p95/p99) with and without affinity hits.
- OOM events and emergency fallbacks triggered.

Test plan: 
- Unit tests for allocator invariants, eviction correctness, and replication bounds.
- Integration tests with the serving runtime simulating pipeline/tensor topologies.
- Stress tests with synthetic long-context skewed workloads and mixed sequence-length batches.
- A/B tests in canary pool with telemetry and automatic rollback criteria (OOM spike, >10% tail latency regression, or >3% increase in token cost).

Rollout plan: 
- Feature gated behind runtime-1.20 flag. Canary starts in a small dedicated pool for internal traffic. If stable, expand to 10% of hosted traffic then to 100% of dedicated pools with controlled monitoring windows.
- Incremental defaults: begin with conservative stripe sizes and replication off; enable replication after stable run.

Open questions / Risks: 
- Optimal stripe size and replication thresholds will vary by model size and batch distribution; need auto-tuning heuristics.
- Metadata overhead and counter accuracy under high QPS. Consider sampling-based hot-key detection.
- Interaction with model quantization and kernel selection (some kernels assume contiguous KV layout). We may need compatibility layers or temporary copies.

Related artifacts and links:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/RUNTIME/Striped+KV+Allocation+Design
- Simulator PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4021 (draft)
- Initial allocator prototype: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4077 (WIP)

- Lock stripe size range and compaction heuristics for the first prototype.
- Begin runtime scheduler hook PR and coordinate with packet/batching owners (cc @Sofia Martinez, @Ravi Patel).

title: Gateway burst-window parsing regression caused 429 wave — follow-up & remediation
preview: Summary: On 2026-02-25 15:12 UTC a configuration parsing change deployed to the API gateway caused the per-tenant burst window field (burst_window_ms) to be interpreted as seconds instead of milliseconds. This produced a 1000x reduction in effective burst window for many customers, rapidly triggering quota enforcement and producing a sustained 429 storm across multiple regions for ~45 minutes. The incident impacted request success rates for hosted API customers and triggered automated retries that amplified load on internal services.

title: Batch-state preemption corruption causing intermittent token duplication and p95 latency spikes
preview: Summary: During a regional capacity reclaim event (preemption cycle) the serving runtime's batching state occasionally became inconsistent between the scheduler and worker kernels. This manifested as duplicated tokens in responses for a subset of requests and transient p95/p99 latency spikes on affected hosts. The behavior appears when preemption interrupts an in-flight batch while KV-cache warmup and kernel handoff are occurring. Impact: Intermittent user-facing incorrect token output and latency regressions for ~0.8% of requests on impacted nodes over a 6-hour window. Scope: Hosted API customers in region us-west-2 on runtime-1.22 serving certain GPT-style models with mixed-precision kernels and autoscaling enabled.

title: Prefill boundary detector and microbenchmark swap policy for safer op-choice transitions
preview:
Problem: For mixed prefill/decode traffic we observe cases where switching to a lower-latency kernel mid-request (typically after a prefill boundary) reduces tail latency but can regress correctness or spike p99 when KV warming and memory layout expectations differ. Current heuristics are brittle across architectures and sequence-length distributions.

Proposal: Implement a two-part runtime subsystem: (1) a lightweight prefill-boundary detector that tags requests where the runtime can safely attempt an op-variant swap after prefill (based on token-phase, recent seqlen histogram, and active KV cache state), and (2) a background microbenchmarking service that continuously produces per-(model,arch,seq-len-bucket) swap safety scores derived from short, sandboxed probes. The runtime will consult both signals to decide a soft-swap policy: allow trial switches for a small probe budget, monitor correctness/latency metrics, and either promote the swap path or roll back and blacklist if regressions occur.

Key ideas: lightweight boundary detection avoids probing on short/predictable dialogs; microbench probes run off critical paths and use isolated memory contexts; soft-swap uses warmup KV replay and can fall back intra-request with no user-visible error; we track both latency regressions and deviation in deterministic outputs (hash-based diffs for deterministic ops), with stricter thresholds for non-deterministic ops.

Why unique: instead of a pure heuristic or purely offline autotune, this combines per-request boundary signals with live microbench-derived confidence scores to allow safe, incremental adoption of faster op-variants per-arch and per-seqlen bucket.

title: Hybrid-Minute Reservation Bands, Soft-Preempt Policy, and SLA Tier Pilot
preview:
Context: Enterprise customers request more granular reserved capacity options to better match variable workloads (short bursts and minute-level spikes) without paying for long-duration commitments. Current committed-hour SKU is too coarse for many ML infra patterns (jobs with 1-30 minute peaks).

Hypothesis: Introducing minute-level reservation bands (e.g., 15, 60, 180 minute commit bands) combined with a soft-preempt policy and a small overage credit pool will increase conversion from trial to committed customers by 8-12% among mid-market accounts and reduce short-term overprovision requests, while preserving predictable revenue.

Proposal: Run a 12-week pilot offering a new SKU family: Hybrid-Minute. Key elements:
- Reservation bands sold as bundles of guaranteed-minutes per month (e.g., 10k x 15m blocks, 4k x 60m blocks).
- Soft-preempt policy: when a reserved minute is consumed, the system will throttle non-reserved jobs within same tenant before preempting reserved slots; preemption only occurs when contention persists >60s.
- Overprovision protection credits: each subscription includes a small pool of 'grace credits' that cover minute-level overages at a reduced rate and are replenished monthly.
- SLA tiers: Bronze (best-effort), Silver (99.5 latency SLO for <2s p95), Gold (99.9 latency SLO + 1hr incident response SLA).
- Admin console controls: visibility into minute-budget consumption, one-click fine-tune to convert spent min-bands into short-term burst buys.

Objectives:
- Reduce ad-hoc burst requests to support and sales by 30% for pilot customers.
- Demonstrate unit economics at scale (target per-minute cost within 25% of existing per-hour reserved SKU).

Scope: Pricing model, runtime preemption & throttle rules, billing & metering changes, console UI, legal addendum for SLA tiers, and enterprise security questionnaire updates. Excluded: Dedicated physical machine redesign or new hardware procurement.

title: Adaptive Tempo Buckets: cadence-aware roadmap scorer for inference economics
preview: Problem: Standard RICE scoring treats effort and impact as static inputs; for inference platform work we see strong time-to-value and capacity-regime effects (short fast wins vs long platform bets), and token-cost/latency sensitivity that varies by workload. Goal: design a practical, repeatable prioritization model that (a) augments RICE with a tempo dimension, (b) applies workload-aware multipliers for token economics and latency risk, and (c) is cycle-aware so scores decay/refresh with cadence. Deliverables: 1) Scoring rubric and spreadsheet prototype (tempo buckets: Sprint, Quarter, Strategic) combining Reach/Impact/Confidence/Effort with Tempo weight and Econ multipliers; 2) Example scoring for 12 candidate initiatives across inference, dedicated, and console; 3) Implementation guidance for PMs and Intake form changes; 4) Small pilot in next cycle to validate alignment with capacity and SLO tradeoffs. Key ideas: - Tempo buckets: Sprint (<=1 cycle, high churn tolerance), Quarter (1-3 cycles), Strategic (multi-cycle). Each bucket applies a 'tempo multiplier' that increases priority for short-window high-impact projects and reduces for long bets unless strategic signals present. - Econ multipliers: calculate expected tokens affected * estimated unit-cost delta * latency-sensitivity multiplier to produce a "cost-risk" value that reduces net impact score when negative. - Capacity regimes: tag initiatives with expected capacity regime (elastic, reserved, private) and apply scaling factors for feasibility/queueing risk. - Cycle decay: scores reduce by X% per cycle unless refreshed by new signals (customer requests, incidents, KPI regressions). Implementation notes: spreadsheet is intentionally transparent; we prefer explicit numeric multipliers and worked examples instead of black-box models. Engineering asks: need telemetry (per-route token volumes, latency cohorts) and a small simulation runner to project unit-econ impact. Success metrics: PM alignment time reduced, fewer re-prioritization meetings, and roadmap experiments delivering expected unit-econ wins in pilot cohort.

title: SLA-driven circuit breaker and staged backpressure for console metrics ingest
preview: Problem: spikes in customer console traffic (bulk exports, aggressive dashboards) are occasionally violating downstream SLOs for per-route P95 latency and causing downstream processors to OOM or fall into retry storms. Existing ingest admission is binary (accept/reject) and causes sharp client-side errors and noisy retries. Goal: build an SLA-driven circuit-breaker and staged backpressure system in the realtime metrics ingest pipeline that can: (1) slow or shed low-value events, (2) gracefully degrade to sampling mode, (3) signal back upstream with clear retry-after semantics, and (4) automatically recover when capacity returns. This ticket covers design, prototype implementation in the ingress proxy + stream preprocessor, and a controlled rollout to select tenants.

title: Microbatch Accordion Binner with Kernel Hinting and Prefill-aware Trimming
preview: Goal: Reduce padding waste and CPU/GPU kernel mismatch penalties for diverse sequence-length workloads by introducing an 'accordion' bucketing strategy that dynamically resizes buckets at runtime and surfaces kernel-hint metadata to the scheduler. Background: Current static bucketing and length-based coalescers still suffer in high-variance traffic (chatty short messages mixed with long contexts) — we pay token and kernel inefficiency (suboptimal kernel selection, poor occupancy) when many microbatches pad to long seqlen. Approach: 1) Accordion Buckets: buckets are parameterized by a compressibility factor; short-heavy tails cause nearby buckets to collapse (narrow width) while long-tail regions expand, creating an accordion-like histogram that adapts every N seconds. 2) Kernel Hint Score: each incoming request computes a lightweight kernel hint (int8 score) indicating preferred kernel path (e.g., fused attention, flash attention, sparse-aware) based on seqlen and sparse-attn probability. The scheduler uses hints to place microbatches on GPU lanes with matching kernel availability. 3) Prefill-aware Trimming: during prefill, detect repeat-prefix-heavy requests and opportunistically trim cached prefixes to reduce prefill length; the runtime tags prefill operations with TTL and recency to avoid cache poisoning. 4) Graceful latency control: for latency-sensitive routes, the accordion binner biases to smaller buckets with faster kernel choices and accepts some throughput loss; for cost-oriented routes, it prioritizes pad reduction and higher occupancy. Metrics and targets: - 15–25% token-padding reduction on mixed traffic traces (measured by pad_delta) - 7–12% per-token GPU time reduction for Web-chat customers - Maintain P95 latency within current SLO for latency-priority routes Implementation plan: - Phase A (PoC): simulate accordion bucketing offline on 30 days of prod traces, implement kernel-hint encoder in runtime (C++/Rust hot-path). (Owner: Marcus) - Phase B (Canary): integrate into prefill pipeline with canary on a small percentage of traffic, add metrics (pad_ratio, kernel-mismatch-rate, slot-occupancy). (Owner: Marcus + Runtime team) - Phase C (Rollout): policy knobs in Console (bucket aggressiveness, kernel-hint weight, prefill-trim threshold), docs and SDK hints. Risks & trade-offs: - Kernel hint noise can misroute and cause more cold kernel invocations; mitigate with smoothing window and fallback. - Prefill trimming risks correctness for apps relying on exact prefix semantics; default off for routes with strict determinism. - Complexity increases scheduler decision space; add guard rails and A/B evaluation. Success criteria: measurable pad reduction with neutral or improved latency for P1 routes; dashboarded regression alerts for any increase in tail latency or error rates.

title: Quantile Warranty & Autoscale Coordination for Dedicated Serve Capacity
preview:
Problem statement: enterprise customers need clear, measurable guarantees for dedicated GPU pools. Current SLA drafts use single-point targets (e.g., tokens/min) and simple credit tables that don't reflect bursty real-world traffic or staggered degradation. This ticket defines a quantile-driven warranty (p95/p99 windows + AUC degradation) and ties it to autoscaler interlocks, customer notices, and contract crediting language.

Scope:
- Define measurable SLA metrics (quantiles, sustained shortfalls, AUC-based crediting).
- Specify autoscaler guardrails and interlock logic to avoid oscillation between autoscale and remediation credits.
- Draft customer-facing contract clauses (notice windows, remediation steps, credit computation appendix).
- Produce monitoring dashboards and alerting thresholds needed to support legal measurement.

Out of scope: per-tenant billing/credits plumbing and final legal sign-off (legal will review after product draft).

title: Operator Rate Sculptor — Preset Chains, Emergency Holds, and Provenance Timeline
preview:
This ticket scopes the Console UI feature 'Operator Rate Sculptor' — a set of fine-grained fleet controls for Dedicated capacity customers that lets operators create chained scaling presets, place emergency holds (throttle/freeze), and inspect a provenance timeline for every control change. The goal is to provide deterministic throughput guarantees while giving SRE/operators clear rollback and auditability instruments.

Problem statement:
- Operators currently lack a single surfaced workflow to create repeatable scaling presets and to temporarily throttle fleets during incidents.
- Change history is fragmented across infra logs and audit tables; Console needs an integrated provenance timeline with context (who, why, linked runbooks/PRs).

This ticket covers UX flows, acceptance criteria, and a phased rollout with toggles for enterprise customers.

The goal is to provide deterministic throughput guarantees while giving SRE/operators clear rollback and auditability instruments.

Problem statement:
- Operators currently lack a single surfaced workflow to create repeatable scaling presets and to temporarily throttle fleets during incidents.
- Change history is fragmented across infra logs and audit tables; Console needs an integrated provenance timeline with context (who, why, linked runbooks/PRs).

This ticket covers UX flows, acceptance criteria, and a phased rollout with toggles for enterprise customers.

title: Geo-Request Classification Engine and SLA-Aware Routing for Enterprise Onboarding
preview: Launch a classification + routing service that automatically triages enterprise region/residency requests into a prioritized intake queue and assigns an SLA-aware routing policy (e.g., local-region, cross-border with contractual exceptions, failover-only). The engine will consume customer intake forms, contract constraints, and operational capacity signals to recommend: priority bucket, target region(s), failover mapping, estimated provisioning lead time, and a minimal set of compliance controls required. This will reduce manual adjudication overhead for Sales/RevOps/Onboarding and improve time-to-provision for high-value customers.

title: Jurisdictional geofence routing with tiered fallback model variants and allow/deny overrides
preview:
Summary: Implement a jurisdictional geofence routing layer that enforces customer-configurable residency and country-level allowlists/denylists, supports tier-aware route selection (hosted/dedicated/private), and performs deterministic fallback to privacy-preserving model variants when hard routing cannot be satisfied. This ticket covers runtime enforcement points, control-plane policy schema, audit logging, and a staged rollout plan for EU/Schengen compliance customers.

Background: Customers in regulated regions require both strict data residency guarantees and the ability to opt their requests into or out of specific jurisdictions. Current routing primitives (region hints + simple region denylists) are insufficient for operator-defined allow/deny policies, tier-aware capacity constraints, and predictable graceful degradation when regional capacity is saturated. Legal and Sales have requested a per-customer configurable policy with strong audit trails and a predictable fallback path that avoids cross-border data movement.

Goals:
- Define a policy schema that supports: country-level allow/deny, preferred-region list (ordered), tier routing rules (e.g., hosted -> dedicated -> private), and a fallback strategy mapping to model variants (e.g., same-architecture quantized variant, on-prem variant, or soft-block).
- Implement control-plane validation and UI hooks in Console for policy editing + preview.
- Enforce policies at the smart-routing proxy (edge) with fast path checks and a control-plane cache for low-latency decisions.
- Provide an auditable event stream (immutable events) for each enforcement decision tied to request IDs, including which fallback executed and why.
- Deliver a rollout plan (pilot customers, canary regions, monitoring knobs).

Design considerations / trade-offs:
- Enforcement location: edge enforcement (lower cross-border risk, higher complexity) vs. control-plane only (simpler but riskier if control-plane-to-edge lag occurs). Decision: primary enforcement at edge with a short-lived signed policy token from control plane; control plane remains source of truth.
- Fallback semantics: soft-fallback (serve with warning and telemetry) vs hard-block (return HTTP 403). For high-regulatory contracts, default is hard-block; for general PLG customers, soft-fallback is allowed.
- Tier routing: prefer matching customer tier (e.g., dedicated) if available in-region; otherwise, fall back to hosted in-region; if neither available, follow fallback mapping to model variants rather than cross-border routing.
- Model variants: prefer privacy-preserving variants that are certified for cross-border use (e.g., reduced-context synthetic model, or on-prem variant deployment). This implies coordination with model onboarding and runtime support for variant mapping.

Technical approach (high level):
1) Policy schema (control plane): JSON schema supporting allowlist, denylist, preferredRegions (ordered), tierPreferences, fallbackMap (ordered list of fallback actions), audit_level. Example: {allowCountries: ["DE","FR"], denyCountries: ["US"], preferredRegions: ["eu-west-1"], tierPreferences: ["dedicated","hosted"], fallbackMap: ["model-variant:privacy-v2","onprem:customer-override","hard-block"]}.
2) Control-plane: UI + API endpoints to create/preview/validate policies; generates signed short-lived policy token (TTL 5m) for edge use.
3) Edge runtime: integrate policy checker into routing proxy. Checks request metadata (customer_id, route, model), consults token and local cache, decides destination or a fallback. Emits structured audit event to Kafka with request_id, policy_key, decision, reason, selected_model, selected_region.
4) Fallback execution: if selected fallback is a model variant, route to variant and attach metadata header X-Redwood-Fallback:privacy-v2. If fallback is onprem and customer has Private deployment, route to private control-plane path. If hard-block, return 403 + structured error code.
5) Observability: dashboards for fallback rates by customer/region, per-policy blocked requests, telemetry for token cache hit/miss, SLO: decision latency p95 < 8ms on edge.

Acceptance criteria:
- Policy schema merged and documented in Confluence (tech spec).
- Control-plane API and Console policy editor prototype in dev cluster.
- Edge policy enforcement integrated into canary proxy and validated in QA with synthetic traffic (test matrix attached).
- Audit stream contains immutable events with the required fields and is consumable by Compliance ETL.
- End-to-end tests that cover allowlist, denylist, tier routing, and each fallback type.
- Legal & Security sign-off on export of telemetry and on fallback behaviors for sample customers.

Links & artifacts:
- Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INF/Jurisdictional+Geofence+Spec
- Design mockups: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/geofence-ui-preview
- Spike PR (routing prototype): https://github.com/redwood-inference/proxy/pull/2745
- Audit consumer consumer config: https://github.com/redwood-inference/compliance-configs/pull/102

Risks & mitigation:
- Risk: Token replay / policy drift between control plane and edge. Mitigation: short TTL tokens, token binding to edge instance id, and automated reconciliation job that compares control-plane policy hash vs edge cache version.
- Risk: Unexpected fallback causing latency spikes. Mitigation: pre-warm fallback model variants, throttle fallback concurrency, and telemetry alerting when fallback rate > 0.5% per-customer in 5m.

Rollout plan:
- Week 0: Merge policy schema and control-plane token endpoint behind feature flag.
- Week 1: Enable edge enforcement in canary region (eu-west-1) for internal accounts.
- Week 2: Pilot with 2 compliant customers (legal-approved) with monitoring and manual rollback plan.
- Week 4: Gradual ramp to all EU customers with email notice and console flag to preview policy.

Notes: This ticket coordinates closely with ENG-31244 (geo-circuit-breaker) and ENG-27845 (tier-fallback policy). Product will provide a simple default policy for new EU customers to reduce support overhead.

title: Cross-Variant Tooling Parity Scorer and Ops Runbook
preview: Build a lightweight, automated compatibility scorer and operational runbook that grades model variants for tokenizer/context/tooling parity and ties score thresholds into smart routing policies. This ticket creates: (1) a parity 'scorecard' that quantifies tokenizer and KV/cache/context compatibility across model variants; (2) a nightly scanner that updates the scorecard and emits metrics; (3) an ops runbook and gating guidance for automating fallback and for manual remediation when parity drops below thresholds.

title: Epochal batch quietude: slot lockups, priority inversion and remediation playbook
preview: Summary: On 2026-02-19 we observed repeated multi-minute periods where continuous batch formation across a subset of serving pods dropped to near-zero ('quietude'), producing tail latency spikes for latency-sensitive customers and an apparent unfairness where lower-priority tenants consumed disproportionate slots after the quiet window. This ticket captures the incident drive, root cause analysis, immediate mitigations, and a concrete playbook of action items to prevent recurrence.

title: Runtime <-> Simulator drift causing allowance calculation mismatch
preview: Summary: We discovered a reproducible discrepancy between the decisions produced by the offline rate-policy simulator (used for QA and rollout validation) and the live runtime evaluator. The simulator reports identical traffic as ALLOW while the runtime occasionally DENYs the same request contexts under identical inputs. This causes confidence drift for policy rollouts and has caused two near-miss incidents where canary traffic was incorrectly classified as safe to increase capacity. Root cause appears to be a small normalization mismatch and different tie-breaking logic between the simulator's policy preprocessor and the runtime policy engine.

title: Batching + KV-cache aliasing causing silent accuracy drift: mitigation, canary expansion, and rollout plan
preview:
Summary: During a production capacity scale event on 2026-02-09 we observed a gradual accuracy drift for a small set of quantized 8-bit model variants serving chat completions. The drift manifested as higher semantic hallucination rates and several customer-reported classification errors. The incident did not cause service-wide crash loops but produced correlated degraded outputs for multi-tenant workloads that used continuous batching.

What happened: Autoscaling and burst batching combined with the runtime's KV-cache prefetch path allowed requests from different model variants (same architecture but different quantization profiles) to reuse prefetched KV entries under certain race conditions. This KV aliasing resulted in silent state leakage across requests, biasing attention for subsequent tokens and producing subtle-but-measurable output drift. The bug is deterministic under a specific composition of: batched requests >2 tenants, mix of model variants (int8 + int4 candidate), and aggressive prefetch enabled by the batching heuristic.

Detection: The degradation was first flagged by a customer alert (classification regression) at 2026-02-09 22:13 UTC and confirmed by increased A/B eval failures on the quality-monitoring dashboard (alert: QUALITY_REGRESSION_QUANT_BATCHING). Canary coverage was limited to single-tenant scenarios and did not exercise mixed-variant batching, so the canary pass was green.

Impact: Affected a narrow subset (~1.6% of overall requests) but disproportionately affected workloads that rely on precise token-level logits (few-shot classification, reranking). No PII exposure or data leak. No persistent corruption of model artifacts; issue was runtime-state only.

Root cause (short): KV-cache prefetch logic reuses the same backing buffer for prefetched key/value blobs and does not fully isolate cache entries by model-variant fingerprint. Under concurrent batching, the buffer can be populated for request A then observed by request B before B applies its own quant-decompress path, leading to a mismatch between quant-format metadata and buffer contents. This mismatch does not always throw an op-fallback; it can silently produce incorrect dequantization and attention key/values that bias generation.

Engineering diagnosis links: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/QUANT-BATCH-KV-INVESTIGATION, https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/ops/quality-regression-quant-batching?orgId=1

title: Retention + redaction rules for audit fields (PII/secret safety + residency behavior)
preview:
Goal
Implement and validate a consistent set of redaction + retention rules for audit log fields across Hosted/Dedicated/Private, ensuring we never log secrets and we minimize PII. This ticket covers:
- Field-level data classification + handling requirements for audit events (v1)
- Redaction/hashing patterns enforced in producers and exporters
- Residency/retention behavior documented and verified (what stays in-region, what gets exported, TTLs)
- Tests + CI guardrails to prevent regressions (secrets-in-logs)

Why (customer + compliance)
- Enterprise security reviews are asking for explicit statements on what we log, how we avoid secrets/PII, and how long audit logs are retained (and where). This blocks Private deals.
- SOC2/ISO evidence expects: auditable actions recorded, restricted access, tamper-resistant-ish pipeline, retention policy, and privacy-safe logging.

References / artifacts
- Canonical schema + field dictionary: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-log-schema-v1.json
- Redaction & data classification draft doc: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-logging-redaction-and-data-classification.json
- Observability standard (emission requirements, cardinality constraints): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/audit-log-events-logging-standard.json
- Versioning/backcompat policy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-log-versioning-and-backcompat-policy.json
- Related customer-reported issues:
  - SUP-18477 (inconsistent fields / normalization)
  - SUP-18510 (duplicates; guidance depends on stable event_id + no secrets)

Scope
IN SCOPE
1) Data classification for every schema v1 field
- Explicit tags: PUBLIC | INTERNAL | CONFIDENTIAL | RESTRICTED (PII) | SECRET
- Handling rules per tag (allowed/forbidden, redaction strategy, retention constraints)

2) Redaction rules and patterns
- Ensure these NEVER appear in audit logs:
  - API keys, bearer tokens, JWTs, session cookies
  - Private keys / cert private material
  - Customer secrets used for SIEM exports (HEC tokens, Sentinel shared keys)
  - Full email addresses in free-form strings (unless explicitly required field)
- Standard redaction patterns:
  - tokens/keys: replace with "[REDACTED]" (do not partially log prefixes)
  - identifiers that are useful for investigation but sensitive (e.g., external_user_id): hash with stable salt per-tenant where applicable (see hashing notes below)
  - IP addresses: allowed in source_ip (CONFIDENTIAL); do not include in arbitrary metadata

3) Retention + residency behavior
- Define and document:
  - Default audit retention in Redwood-managed Hosted/Dedicated (e.g., 90d standard; longer via add-on)
  - Private deployment defaults (configurable retention; local storage; customer-controlled)
  - Residency: audit logs should follow tenant residency settings for storage and ingestion; exporter can send out-of-region only under explicit customer configuration
  - Redaction should happen BEFORE events leave the producing service boundary (best effort), and exporter must re-validate/sanitize as a second layer

4) Enforcement + verification
- Producer library helpers:
  - Safe-string / safe-metadata helpers (denylist patterns)
  - Structured field setters that prevent leaking secrets into "details" / "metadata" / "diff"
- Exporter validation:
  - Validate against schema v1 and run redaction sanitizer on output
- Tests:
  - Unit tests for sanitizer patterns
  - Integration test fixture with known-bad payloads (token-like strings) that must be redacted
- CI:
  - Add log-scanning test suite in audit library (or reuse existing secrets scanners) for audit event payload serialization

OUT OF SCOPE
- Building a customer-facing UI for audit browsing
- Redacting application logs outside the audit stream (handled by separate logging policy)

Design decisions / requirements
A) Hashing strategy (when redaction would remove all utility)
- Use hashing only for fields that are (1) sensitive and (2) required for correlation across events.
- Proposed: HMAC-SHA256(value, tenant_audit_salt) and store salt in KMS (Hosted/Dedicated) or customer-provided secret (Private).
- Output format: "hmac_sha256:<hex>" (to make it explicit this is not raw data).
- Open question: do we need cross-tenant correlation? Answer should be no.

B) Diff summaries
- Many sensitive actions include a "diff". Requirement:
  - diff must only include allow-listed keys and must NOT include free-form values (e.g., do not dump entire policy JSON)
  - include "changed_fields" list and a small "before/after" for bounded enums/booleans/integers
  - for strings: include "[REDACTED]" unless explicitly allow-listed + bounded

C) Field-level retention
- Some fields may require shorter retention than the event as a whole (e.g., source_ip for certain regions) but implementing per-field TTL is heavy.
- Decision (proposal): keep single event TTL; minimize sensitive values via hashing/redaction so the entire event is safe for standard retention windows.

Acceptance criteria
- Confluence doc updated with:
  1) field-by-field classification table for schema v1
  2) explicit "MUST NOT LOG" list
  3) redaction/hashing rules and examples
  4) retention defaults and configuration surfaces (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
  5) residency behavior statement, including exporter caveats
- Shared audit library includes sanitizer helpers and tests; at least 2 producer services updated to use helpers (Control Plane + Console backend preferred)
- Exporter validates + sanitizes outgoing events (second layer)
- Regression test suite contains at least 10 known-bad cases (JWT-like, api_key-like, HEC token-like, cookie-like, PEM-like strings) and proves output contains "[REDACTED]" and no token substrings
- Sign-off from Privacy/Legal (Michael Grant) + GRC (Olga Petrov) on externally communicable statements

Implementation plan (proposed)
1) Finalize classification + rules doc
- Owner: Hanae Suzuki
- Reviewers: Michael Grant (privacy/legal), Olga Petrov (GRC), Allison Grant (observability standard)

2) Implement sanitizer in schema library
- Add a function sanitizeAuditEvent(event) called:
  - by producers before enqueue
  - by exporter before emit
- Include:
  - key-pattern detection (JWT header "eyJ", PEM headers, common api_key prefixes)
  - header/cookie field denylist if any services pass HTTP headers (they shouldn’t)
  - safeDiff(allowlist) helper

3) Wire sanitizer into at least two services + exporter
- Control plane: RBAC + API key lifecycle events
- Console backend: export config changes + retention settings
- Exporter: enforce sanitize + schema validate

4) Tests + CI
- Add test vectors + snapshot tests
- Add a CI check that fails if any test vector leaks raw secrets (string match + regex)

Rollout / risk
- Risk: over-redaction reduces investigation value; mitigate with targeted hashing and bounded diffs.
- Risk: breaking customers parsing legacy fields; mitigate by keeping v1 stable and ensuring exporter normalization (ENG-4827/ENG-4833).
- Risk: Private deployments have different retention/storage backends; mitigate by documenting defaults and validating config surfaces in private installer workstream.

Open questions
- Do we treat user email as PII and always hash? Proposal: store email only when it is the authenticated principal (actor_email) and keep it as CONFIDENTIAL (not SECRET); do not allow email in generic metadata fields.
- Exact default retention window for Hosted/Dedicated (90d vs 180d). Need Naomi + GRC alignment; depends on packaging.

Progress updates
- 2026-02-12 (Hanae): Drafted field classification table; flagged two high-risk areas: diff payloads for policy updates and exporter destination config values.
- 2026-02-18 (Kevin): Exporter already has validation hooks; adding sanitize step is straightforward. Need final rules doc to implement denylist patterns consistently.
- 2026-02-24 (Allison): Observability standard updated to explicitly ban free-form "details" blobs for sensitive actions; require bounded fields.
- 2026-02-27 (Hanae): PR in progress for sanitizer helpers + tests. Pending Michael (privacy/legal) review on retention/residency wording.

Notes for Support/Solutions (customer-facing)
- We can state:
  - Redwood audit logs are designed to avoid secrets; token/key material is never logged.
  - Audit events include stable identifiers (event_id, actor, target) and bounded diffs; sensitive values are redacted/hmac-hashed.
  - Retention defaults and residency behavior are configurable; exporter only sends out-of-region when customer configures it.
- Do not claim exactly-once delivery or guaranteed zero duplicates (see delivery semantics ADR).

- Versioning/backcompat policy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-log-versioning-and-backcompat-policy.json
- Related customer-reported issues:
  - SUP-18477 (inconsistent fields / normalization)
  - SUP-18510 (duplicates; guidance depends on stable event_id + no secrets)

Scope
IN SCOPE
1) Data classification for every schema v1 field
- Explicit tags: PUBLIC | INTERNAL | CONFIDENTIAL | RESTRICTED (PII) | SECRET
- Handling rules per tag (allowed/forbidden, redaction strategy, retention constraints)

2) Redaction rules and patterns
- Ensure these NEVER appear in audit logs:
  - API keys, bearer tokens, JWTs, session cookies
  - Private keys / cert private material
  - Customer secrets used for SIEM exports (HEC tokens, Sentinel shared keys)
  - Full email addresses in free-form strings (unless explicitly required field)
- Standard redaction patterns:
  - tokens/keys: replace with "[REDACTED]" (do not partially log prefixes)
  - identifiers that are useful for investigation but sensitive (e.g., external_user_id): hash with stable salt per-tenant where applicable (see hashing notes below)
  - IP addresses: allowed in source_ip (CONFIDENTIAL); do not include in arbitrary metadata

3) Retention + residency behavior
- Define and document:
  - Default audit retention in Redwood-managed Hosted/Dedicated (e.g., 90d standard; longer via add-on)
  - Private deployment defaults (configurable retention; local storage; customer-controlled)
  - Residency: audit logs should follow tenant residency settings for storage and ingestion; exporter can send out-of-region only under explicit customer configuration
  - Redaction should happen BEFORE events leave the producing service boundary (best effort), and exporter must re-validate/sanitize as a second layer

4) Enforcement + verification
- Producer library helpers:
  - Safe-string / safe-metadata helpers (denylist patterns)
  - Structured field setters that prevent leaking secrets into "details" / "metadata" / "diff"
- Exporter validation:
  - Validate against schema v1 and run redaction sanitizer on output
- Tests:
  - Unit tests for sanitizer patterns
  - Integration test fixture with known-bad payloads (token-like strings) that must be redacted
- CI:
  - Add log-scanning test suite in audit library (or reuse existing secrets scanners) for audit event payload serialization

OUT OF SCOPE
- Building a customer-facing UI for audit browsing
- Redacting application logs outside the audit stream (handled by separate logging policy)

Design decisions / requirements
A) Hashing strategy (when redaction would remove all utility)
- Use hashing only for fields that are (1) sensitive and (2) required for correlation across events.
- Proposed: HMAC-SHA256(value, tenant_audit_salt) and store salt in KMS (Hosted/Dedicated) or customer-provided secret (Private).
- Output format: "hmac_sha256:<hex>" (to make it explicit this is not raw data).
- Open question: do we need cross-tenant correlation? Answer should be no.

B) Diff summaries
- Many sensitive actions include a "diff". Requirement:
  - diff must only include allow-listed keys and must NOT include free-form values (e.g., do not dump entire policy JSON)
  - include "changed_fields" list and a small "before/after" for bounded enums/booleans/integers
  - for strings: include "[REDACTED]" unless explicitly allow-listed + bounded

C) Field-level retention
- Some fields may require shorter retention than the event as a whole (e.g., source_ip for certain regions) but implementing per-field TTL is heavy.
- Decision (proposal): keep single event TTL; minimize sensitive values via hashing/redaction so the entire event is safe for standard retention windows.

Acceptance criteria
- Confluence doc updated with:
  1) field-by-field classification table for schema v1
  2) explicit "MUST NOT LOG" list
  3) redaction/hashing rules and examples
  4) retention defaults and configuration surfaces (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
  5) residency behavior statement, including exporter caveats
- Shared audit library includes sanitizer helpers and tests; at least 2 producer services updated to use helpers (Control Plane + Console backend preferred)
- Exporter validates + sanitizes outgoing events (second layer)
- Regression test suite contains at least 10 known-bad cases (JWT-like, api_key-like, HEC token-like, cookie-like, PEM-like strings) and proves output contains "[REDACTED]" and no token substrings
- Sign-off from Privacy/Legal (Michael Grant) + GRC (Olga Petrov) on externally communicable statements

Implementation plan (proposed)
1) Finalize classification + rules doc
- Owner: Hanae Suzuki
- Reviewers: Michael Grant (privacy/legal), Olga Petrov (GRC), Allison Grant (observability standard)

2) Implement sanitizer in schema library
- Add a function sanitizeAuditEvent(event) called:
  - by producers before enqueue
  - by exporter before emit
- Include:
  - key-pattern detection (JWT header "eyJ", PEM headers, common api_key prefixes)
  - header/cookie field denylist if any services pass HTTP headers (they shouldn’t)
  - safeDiff(allowlist) helper

3) Wire sanitizer into at least two services + exporter
- Control plane: RBAC + API key lifecycle events
- Console backend: export config changes + retention settings
- Exporter: enforce sanitize + schema validate

4) Tests + CI
- Add test vectors + snapshot tests
- Add a CI check that fails if any test vector leaks raw secrets (string match + regex)

Rollout / risk
- Risk: over-redaction reduces investigation value; mitigate with targeted hashing and bounded diffs.
- Risk: breaking customers parsing legacy fields; mitigate by keeping v1 stable and ensuring exporter normalization (ENG-4827/ENG-4833).
- Risk: Private deployments have different retention/storage backends; mitigate by documenting defaults and validating config surfaces in private installer workstream.

Open questions
- Do we treat user email as PII and always hash? Proposal: store email only when it is the authenticated principal (actor_email) and keep it as CONFIDENTIAL (not SECRET); do not allow email in generic metadata fields.
- Exact default retention window for Hosted/Dedicated (90d vs 180d). Need Naomi + GRC alignment; depends on packaging.

Progress updates
- 2026-02-12 (Hanae): Drafted field classification table; flagged two high-risk areas: diff payloads for policy updates and exporter destination config values.
- 2026-02-18 (Kevin): Exporter already has validation hooks; adding sanitize step is straightforward. Need final rules doc to implement denylist patterns consistently.
- 2026-02-24 (Allison): Observability standard updated to explicitly ban free-form "details" blobs for sensitive actions; require bounded fields.
- 2026-02-27 (Hanae): PR in progress for sanitizer helpers + tests. Pending Michael (privacy/legal) review on retention/residency wording.

Notes for Support/Solutions (customer-facing)
- We can state:
  - Redwood audit logs are designed to avoid secrets; token/key material is never logged.
  - Audit events include stable identifiers (event_id, actor, target) and bounded diffs; sensitive values are redacted/hmac-hashed.
  - Retention defaults and residency behavior are configurable; exporter only sends out-of-region when customer configures it.
- Do not claim exactly-once delivery or guaranteed zero duplicates (see delivery semantics ADR).

title: Policy engine: emit v1 audit events for routing/policy changes (fallback rules, allow-lists) with diff + approver context
preview:
Problem
Policy/routing changes are classified as “sensitive actions” for SOC2/ISO-aligned auditability and customer incident investigations. Today policy-service emits partial/legacy logs and does not consistently capture (a) canonical event_type, (b) stable diff summaries, and (c) approver context when changes go through console workflows.

Scope (in-scope)
- Emit schema v1 audit events for policy/routing config mutations across Hosted/Dedicated/Private:
  - Fallback rules updates (e.g., primary model -> fallback model variants, region failover behavior)
  - Allow-list / deny-list updates (e.g., model allow-list by tenant/project, routing constraints)
  - Policy set updates (create/update/delete of policy objects; enable/disable)
- Include a diff summary suitable for SIEM ingestion (bounded cardinality, no secrets/PII).
- Include “approver context” where applicable (console-driven or API-driven approvals / 4-eyes flows).
- Register event types in centralized event_type registry and ensure payload validates against audit-log-schema-v1.

Out of scope
- Customer-facing UI changes beyond the existing policy editor workflow.
- Logging full raw policy documents as event payloads.
- Retroactive backfill for historical policy changes prior to schema v1 cutover (handled by exporter normalization ticket where possible).

Event taxonomy (proposed; align to sensitive-actions-event-taxonomy.json)
- policy.routing.updated
- policy.fallback.updated
- policy.allowlist.updated
- policy.policy_set.created
- policy.policy_set.updated
- policy.policy_set.deleted
- policy.policy_set.enabled
- policy.policy_set.disabled

Required fields / semantics (schema v1)
- schema_version: "1.0"
- event_id: ULID (preferred for time ordering) or UUIDv7; must be stable per emitted event
- event_time: RFC3339
- event_type: from registry only (CI lint blocks unknown types)
- outcome: "success" | "failure" (include error_code on failure)
- actor: derived from auth context
  - actor.type: "user" | "service_account" | "system"
  - actor.id / actor.email (email only where already present in auth context; do not add new sources of PII)
  - actor.session_id (if available)
  - impersonation.* if request is via support/operator impersonation (must be explicit)
- target/resource:
  - resource.type: "routing_policy" | "policy_set"
  - resource.id: stable policy identifier
  - resource.name: optional; must be bounded and preferably an internal slug (avoid arbitrary free-form names)
- tenancy context:
  - org_id / tenant_id (as used in control plane)
  - project_id where applicable
  - deployment_mode: "hosted" | "dedicated" | "private"
- correlation:
  - request_id, trace_id
  - admin_action_id when action is initiated from Console workflows
- source context:
  - source_ip (from gateway headers; normalize and validate)
  - user_agent (optional; truncated)

Diff summary requirements (cardinality + redaction safe)
- Must not include:
  - secrets, tokens, full endpoint URLs with embedded credentials, raw customer prompt/content, or user-provided regexes/strings that explode cardinality
- Provide:
  - change_summary: stable, bounded summary fields only (counts + field names from an allow-list)
  - changed_fields: comma-separated list of allow-listed fields (max N=20)
  - diff_fingerprint: sha256 of canonicalized before/after JSON (server-side) to support investigation without emitting full policy
  - before_version / after_version: integer or semver-like policy revision
- For fallbacks specifically:
  - include fallback_chain_length_before/after
  - include whether cross-region fallback toggled (boolean string)

Approver context
- If the mutation was approved via an approval workflow (console change requiring approval, or internal gating):
  - approval_required: "true" | "false"
  - approval_id: identifier from approvals service (if present)
  - approver_actor_id: who approved (may differ from actor if separate approver)
  - approval_reason: short enum or bounded text (truncate; prefer enum)
- If no formal approval exists, populate approval_required="false" and omit approver fields.

Implementation notes
- Hook into policy-service mutation handlers (create/update/delete/enable/disable endpoints) after successful persistence, before responding (best-effort emission but must not block request on audit sink availability per delivery ADR).
- Use shared audit event library + validator (ENG-4821) and event_type registry (ENG-4822).
- Ensure event emission uses the standard correlation propagation (ENG-4830) so investigations can join request logs + traces.
- Add unit tests:
  - verifies event_type mapping per route
  - verifies diff summary is bounded (field allow-list enforced, truncation works)
  - verifies approver context set when admin_action_id + approval metadata present
- Add integration coverage in ENG-4834 for at least:
  - update fallback rule
  - update allow-list
  - delete policy set

Acceptance criteria
- For all policy-service mutation endpoints in scope, a v1 audit event is emitted on success and on failure (with outcome="failure" and error_code).
- Emitted events validate against audit-log-schema-v1.json.
- event_type is registered; CI lint passes with no local overrides.
- Diff summary is present and passes cardinality budget guidance (no raw policy dumps).
- Approver context is included when available and does not misattribute actor vs approver.
- Verified in at least one Private fixture run (simulated via installer/dev env) to confirm deployment_mode tagging + parity.

Links
- Confluence standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/audit-log-events-logging-standard.json
- ADR (schema + taxonomy): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-log-schema-and-event-taxonomy.json
- Related PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-22033-policy-engine-audit-events-for-routing-policy-updates.json

Open questions
- Do we need a separate event_type for “policy validation failed” vs generic outcome=failure? (current plan: keep event_type for attempted action, set outcome=failure + error_code)
- For console drafts vs applied changes: only emit audit events on apply (persist) step; drafts remain non-sensitive and are not audited.

title: Publish audit logging docs + runbooks; verify alert/dashboard links and incident templates
preview:
Goal
Finalize and publish the audit logging documentation set and operational runbooks, then validate that all dashboards/alerts and incident workflows link to the correct sources. This is the “last mile” for enterprise readiness: we need consistent internal guidance (SRE + Support + Solutions) and externally shareable references (schema, delivery semantics, SIEM export guidance) without making roadmap promises.

Scope
- Confluence publish pass: schema, taxonomy, versioning/backcompat, redaction/data classification, logging standard.
- Runbooks publish pass: audit pipeline incident runbook + coverage regression runbook.
- Verify links from:
  - observability dashboards/alerts (drop rate / latency / backlog, coverage dashboard)
  - on-call dashboard widgets
  - incident templates / postmortem templates (where we reference audit pipeline)
- Internal enablement:
  - short “what changed” note for Eng + Support + Sales Eng
  - pointers to troubleshooting + common customer questions (duplicates, backcompat, idempotency)

Out of scope
- Writing new customer-facing UI docs beyond export configuration and schema references.
- Reworking the exporter guide beyond the agreed formats/targets.

  - sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-log-schema-v1.json

  - sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/sensitive-actions-event-taxonomy.json

  - sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-log-schema-and-event-taxonomy.json
  - sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-event-delivery-guarantees.json
- Runbooks:
  - sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
  - sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/audit-log-coverage-regression-runbook.json

Acceptance criteria
1) All Confluence pages above are marked “Published”, have correct owners, and have last-reviewed dates updated.
2) Runbooks are linked from:

- Delivery semantics summary aligns with ADR: at-least-once delivery; duplicates possible; customers must dedupe by event_id.
