title: Console backend: emit v1 audit events for admin actions with consistent actor attribution
preview:
Problem
Console (web) admin workflows currently trigger sensitive actions in multiple backend services (Console backend, Control Plane, Exporter config service). Today we have partial/legacy audit events and inconsistent attribution (sometimes only service identity, missing impersonation, missing source IP/user agent), which breaks investigation workflows and causes inconsistent SIEM fields (ref SUP-18477).

Goal
Ensure Console backend emits canonical Audit Event Schema v1 events for Console-initiated sensitive admin actions (role edits, audit log export config changes, retention/residency settings). Events must follow the v1 schema + taxonomy, and actor attribution must be consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Scope (Console backend)
1) RBAC/admin access changes initiated via Console UI
- Roles: create/update/delete role; role permission edits
- Assignments: add/remove user to role; change role bindings
- Invites: invite user, revoke invite (if supported)

2) Audit log export configuration workflows
- Create/update/delete export destination (Splunk HEC, Sentinel/Log Analytics, generic HTTPS)
- Enable/disable export
- Validate/test export destination (connectivity + auth check) if present

3) Retention / residency settings exposed via Console
- Tenant/project retention policy changes
- Region / residency setting changes (where applicable)
- Log retention changes for audit logs (if configurable per tenant)

Non-goals
- Build customer-facing audit log UI beyond existing basic view (tracked elsewhere)
- Guarantee exactly-once delivery to sinks (covered by pipeline ADR + exporter behavior); we must ensure event_id and idempotency guidance exists

Implementation approach
- Use shared audit event library + validator (ENG-4821 / PR-21984) in Console backend.
- Add an audit-emission helper for admin workflows that:
  - Validates event_type is registered in taxonomy registry (ENG-4822 / PR-21991).
  - Stamps schema_version ("1.0") and emits to the audit ingestion topic/endpoint.
  - Captures actor + request context consistently:
    - actor_type: "user" (Console user) or "service" for automated Console backend actions
    - actor_id: internal user_id (stable) and actor_email (if allowed by classification) OR actor_display where permitted
    - actor_session_id (if available)
    - impersonator_* fields when Console support impersonation is used (support/admin tooling)
    - source_ip, user_agent
    - request_id, trace_id, admin_action_id (ENG-4830)
- Ensure targets/resources are captured consistently:
  - target_type: "role" | "role_binding" | "export_destination" | "retention_policy" | "residency_setting"
  - target_id: stable UUID
  - target_scope: tenant_id + project_id (if applicable)
  - before/after summaries: provide diff summary fields while respecting cardinality + redaction rules (ENG-4832)

Key event_type mappings (must match sensitive-actions taxonomy)
- rbac.role.create / rbac.role.update / rbac.role.delete
- rbac.binding.create / rbac.binding.delete
- audit_export.destination.create / audit_export.destination.update / audit_export.destination.delete
- audit_export.export.enable / audit_export.export.disable
- audit_export.destination.test
- data_retention.policy.update
- data_residency.settings.update

Actor attribution rules (agreed with Security)
- Always attribute the initiating Console user as actor_* when an authenticated human performed the action.
- If action is executed asynchronously (job queue), preserve original actor context via signed/validated admin_action_context.
- If support impersonation is used:
  - actor_* = impersonated user (effective identity)
  - impersonator_* = admin/support user (true origin)
- If action is triggered by system automation (no user): actor_type = "service" and include service_name + deployment_mode.

Delivery / failure handling
- Do not block admin workflows on audit sink availability (best-effort emission with bounded retry), but do:
  - Record a metric + structured error log on failed emission
  - Emit to a local buffer/queue when possible (Console backend) to avoid drops during transient outages
  - Tie into audit pipeline SLOs/alerts (ENG-4829 + ENG-4835)

Testing
- Unit tests: event builder validation, taxonomy enforcement, redaction checks for common payload fields.
- Integration tests: execute Console backend handlers for each sensitive workflow and assert:
  - event_type correct
  - required fields present per schema v1
  - actor attribution present (source_ip/user_agent/request_id)
  - event_id is generated and stable per event
- E2E tests: covered by ENG-4834, but Console backend should provide deterministic hooks in test environment to fetch last emitted audit event for a request_id/admin_action_id.

Acceptance criteria
- For all in-scope workflows, a v1 audit event is emitted with:
  - schema_version present
  - event_type in registry
  - actor attribution fields present (actor_id/type + source_ip + user_agent where available)
  - tenant_id present and correct
  - request_id + trace_id present
  - target_id/type present
- Verified in staging via coverage dashboard (ENG-4828): expected event types observed for Console service at >= 99% over 24h with synthetic runs.
- No secrets/credentials appear in any audit payload (validated via redaction lint + spot checks).

Notes / open questions
- Do we log actor_email in audit events? Current direction: avoid by default; allow exporter normalization to optionally enrich email downstream. Pending confirmation from "audit-logging-redaction-and-data-classification" doc.
- For export destination "test" action: ensure the payload does NOT include endpoint auth headers or secret fragments; only log destination_id + result enum.

References
- Confluence: security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-log-schema-v1.json
- Confluence: architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/audit-log-events-logging-standard.json
- Jira: SUP-18477 (SIEM export contains inconsistent fields)

title: Add CI check: audit events must include required v1 fields (and registered event_type)
preview:
Context
- We’re standardizing audit logs across Hosted/Dedicated/Private with a canonical schema v1 + event taxonomy.
- Instrumentation work is underway service-by-service, but we keep regressing on required fields (missing source_ip, missing actor.id for console actions, missing request_id/trace_id, etc.).
- We need a CI gate that prevents:
  (a) emitting unregistered event_type values
  (b) emitting v1 events missing required fields for a given event_type
  (c) introducing new audited code paths without test coverage hooks (where feasible)

Goal
Add an enforcement step in CI for the monorepo that validates audit event emission sites against:
- the event_type registry (taxonomy)
- schema v1 required fields (global required + per-event required)
- redaction rules (basic pattern checks for secrets/PII where we can statically detect)

Non-goals
- Perfect static analysis across all languages/frameworks. The gate should be pragmatic and catch the most common regressions.
- Blocking on events that are produced exclusively at runtime (e.g., some async processors) without an “expected fields contract” available.

Definition of Done / Acceptance Criteria
1) CI fails if a new/modified audit event emission includes an event_type not in the registry.
2) CI fails if required v1 top-level fields are missing from the emission call OR from the constructed payload:
   - schema_version
   - event_id
   - occurred_at
   - event_type
   - tenant_id (or deployment-scoped equivalent for Private where tenant is not applicable; see exceptions)
   - actor.type and actor.id (or actor.type=system + system identifier)
   - source.ip (required for user/admin actions when present in request context; for Private offline operator flows, see rules below)
3) CI fails if per-event required fields are missing based on taxonomy:
   - e.g., RBAC role update must include target.resource_type=role, target.resource_id, and change_summary (bounded)
   - API key lifecycle events must include target.resource_type=api_key and key_id (never key material)
4) Provide a service-owner template and examples:
   - “How to emit an audit event” snippet
   - “How to add a new event_type” snippet
   - “How to write a unit test that asserts required fields” snippet
5) Provide an exceptions mechanism:
   - explicit allowlist file with expiry + issue link
   - CI fails if an exception is expired
6) Documentation: add an internal doc page/README in repo describing:
   - required fields
   - how the linter works
   - how to debug failures
   - how to request an exception

Proposed Approach (implementation detail)
A) Enforce at the library boundary
- We already have/are landing a shared audit event schema library + validator.
- Add a strict “validated emit” API that requires a typed struct / builder (preferred) rather than arbitrary map payloads.
- Require call sites to use the strict path in audited code paths.

B) CI check structure
- Add a repo CI job: audit-event-lint
- Inputs:
  - event taxonomy registry (checked in)
  - schema v1 (checked in)
  - optional per-service mapping file for cases we can’t infer (legacy producers)
- Checks:
  1) Diff-based scan: if a change touches files matching audit emission patterns, run lint.
  2) Static checks:
     - disallow raw JSON maps passed to emitter (language-specific)
     - require schema_version constant
     - require event_type to be from registry
  3) Test-level checks (where possible):
     - if service has integration tests for sensitive actions, ensure they assert required fields (at least the global required set)

C) Language/stack considerations
- Control plane services: Go
- Console backend: TypeScript/Node
- Private installer tooling: mixed (Go + shell); may not have request context

Rules for edge cases
- Private offline operator flows (install/upgrade/rollback):
  - actor.type may be "operator" or "system"
  - actor.id may be "unknown" ONLY if no auth context is available; must include actor.display_name if operator provided a name
  - source.ip may be omitted when execution context is local/offline; must include source.type="local" and source.hostname when available
- System-initiated actions:
  - actor.type="system" and actor.id="<service-name>" or "scheduler"
- Correlation IDs:
  - request_id and trace_id required when request context exists; if not, must include admin_action_id (or workflow_id) for installer/async operations

Exception mechanism
- Repo file: audit/audit_event_lint_exceptions.yaml
- Each entry requires: owning team, reason, expiration date, and a Linear issue key.
- CI behavior: warn within 7 days of expiration, fail on/after expiration.

Deliverables
- CI job + linter executable/script in repo
- Add pre-commit hook (optional, non-blocking) for faster local feedback
- Service-owner templates in /docs or /audit/README
- Example PR that updates one Go service + one TS service to comply

Open Questions
- Do we block merges on missing source.ip for Console actions while we’re still standardizing gateway/header forwarding?
  - Proposal: block for Go control plane and gateway immediately; warn-only for Console until ENG-4830 propagation is fully landed.
- Should we validate cardinality constraints (allow-lists for event_type, resource_type) in CI?
  - Proposal: yes for enums; no for free-form fields beyond max length checks.

Rollout Plan
1) Land linter in warn-only mode for one week; publish “how to fix” examples.
2) Flip to blocking for: event_type registry + global required fields.
3) After coverage stabilizes, add per-event required fields enforcement (taxonomy-driven) for the top 10 sensitive actions first.

Links / References
- Linear: ENG-4821 (schema v1), ENG-4822 (taxonomy registry), ENG-4830 (correlation IDs), ENG-4832 (redaction rules)
- GitHub (expected): PR for CI gate + lint rules (will link when opened)
- Confluence: Audit log events logging standard (observability standards)

Risks
- False positives causing CI churn.
  - Mitigation: phased rollout, clear error messages, minimal heuristics, explicit exceptions with expiry.
- Teams bypassing strict emitter.
  - Mitigation: forbid direct writes to audit topic/sink; enforce usage via build rules and/or codeowners review.

Task Breakdown
- [ ] Finalize required fields set (global + per-event) with Security (Marcus/Hanae) + Observability (Allison)
- [ ] Implement registry check (unknown event_type fails)
- [ ] Implement required-fields validation for strict emitter
- [ ] Add diff-based CI job + docs + examples
- [ ] Add exceptions mechanism with expiry enforcement
- [ ] Rollout plan + comms in #eng-platform and #eng-security

title: Add integration tests: sensitive actions emit v1 audit events (hosted + private fixtures)
preview:
Context
We are standardizing audit logging across Hosted/Dedicated/Private and gating launch on “100% coverage for sensitive actions.” Unit tests in individual services are not sufficient to prevent regressions (new endpoints / UI flows missing audit emission, missing required fields, inconsistent actor attribution). This ticket adds end-to-end tests that perform representative sensitive actions and assert that a correctly-shaped schema v1 audit event is produced.

Goals
- Catch missing audit events for sensitive actions during CI before merge.
- Validate schema v1 shape + required fields via the shared validator library.
- Validate actor/target semantics and correlation fields (request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id) for investigation workflows.
- Ensure parity: the same action in Hosted fixture and Private fixture yields an equivalent event_type + required fields.

Non-goals
- Building a customer-facing audit log UI.
- Full coverage of every UI click path (we will target canonical backend actions; UI flows are covered via Console backend tests where feasible).
- Validating delivery guarantees end-to-end to external SIEMs (covered by pipeline SLO/alerting and exporter tests). This suite focuses on event production + correctness.

Test strategy (high level)
We implement a small “audit test harness” that can:
1) Execute a sensitive action via the public/admin API surface used by customers (or Console backend endpoint where that is the canonical producer).
2) Capture produced audit events from a deterministic sink in test mode.
3) Assert:
   - event_type is registered / expected
   - schema_version is present and matches v1.x
   - JSON payload validates against schema v1 (using the shared schema library)
   - required fields are present and non-empty (tenant/org/project where applicable)
   - actor is correct (user vs service vs system; impersonation present only when used)
   - no secret/PII leaks (ensure redaction placeholders rather than raw values)
   - correlation IDs align (request_id in API response header matches audit event request_id; trace_id present when tracing enabled)

Deterministic audit sink
To avoid flakiness from async pipelines, the services will support a test-only sink mode:
- Hosted fixture: start control-plane + gateway in “AUDIT_SINK=http” mode, pointing to an in-process collector (test container) that buffers events by (event_id, request_id).
- Private fixture: same collector runs inside the docker-compose/kind stack; configure via installer values or env overrides.

Notes:
- We will not depend on the exporter, Kafka-like queues, or production ingestion during these tests. We only need the event producer to emit successfully.
- We keep strict time bounds: wait up to 5s for the expected event by request_id (polling collector endpoint).

Test matrix (initial set)
The matrix is intentionally limited to the highest-risk, highest-frequency sensitive actions that have historically regressed or are customer-reviewed in security questionnaires.

A) RBAC / access
1. Create role (custom role)
   - Action: POST /rbac/roles
   - Expect: rbac.role.create
2. Update role permissions
   - Action: PATCH /rbac/roles/{id}
   - Expect: rbac.role.update (includes diff_summary)
3. Delete role
   - Action: DELETE /rbac/roles/{id}
   - Expect: rbac.role.delete

B) Credential lifecycle
4. Create API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys
   - Expect: credential.api_key.create
   - Assert: key material is NOT present; only key_id / key_prefix allowed per redaction rules
5. Rotate API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys/{id}/rotate
   - Expect: credential.api_key.rotate
6. Revoke API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys/{id}/revoke
   - Expect: credential.api_key.revoke

C) Policy / routing / rollouts

   - Action: PUT /policy/routing

- Installer upgrade/rollback events are emitted from the installer repo and require a heavier harness. We will add a minimal smoke test in the installer pipeline; not part of this suite (see ENG-4826).

- Hosted docker-compose: configure gateway/control-plane to emit to collector.
- Private fixture: pass AUDIT_SINK_URL via values/env into the stack.

3) Write tests
- Use existing e2e harness (supertest/go test harness depending on suite) to:
  - perform action
  - capture request_id from response headers
  - poll collector until event is present

- Add retry/poll with bounded timeout.

- Tests exist for items 1–10 above and run in CI at least for Hosted.

- Source IP availability in Private (behind internal ingress) may differ; we should assert presence only when expected by producer context.
- Correlation propagation: some actions might be async; those should use admin_action_id instead of request_id. For this ticket we focus on synchronous HTTP actions.
- Flakiness from shared environments: mitigate by using unique tenant per test run.

Non-goals
- Building a customer-facing audit log UI.

1) Execute a sensitive action via the public/admin API surface used by customers (or Console backend endpoint where that is the canonical producer).

   - correlation IDs align (request_id in API response header matches audit event request_id; trace_id present when tracing enabled)

- Hosted fixture: start control-plane + gateway in “AUDIT_SINK=http” mode, pointing to an in-process collector (test container) that buffers events by (event_id, request_id).
- Private fixture: same collector runs inside the docker-compose/kind stack; configure via installer values or env overrides.

   - Expect: data.residency.update

title: Audit event_type registry (enum) + build-time lints for schema-required fields
preview:
We need a centralized, canonical registry of audit event types (taxonomy) and enforcement so engineers cannot:
- emit an unregistered event_type
- omit required schema fields for a given event_type

This ticket delivers:
1) a single event_type registry (enum) aligned to the sensitive-actions taxonomy
2) build-time lint rules that fail CI when an event emitter uses an unknown event_type or misses required fields

This is a prerequisite for credible “100% coverage” claims and for keeping schema semantics stable across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

title: Private installer: emit audit events for install/upgrade/rollback/config apply + credential rotation
preview:
Context
Private customers (esp. regulated) are blocking on “parity” for sensitive actions: they want the same audit semantics in Private deployments as Hosted/Dedicated. Today the Private installer/upgrade tooling performs critical actions (install, upgrade, rollback, config apply, credential rotation) but does not emit consistently shaped audit events, making investigations and SOC2 evidence collection difficult.

Goal
Instrument the redwood-private-installer and related upgrade tooling to emit canonical audit events (schema v1) for the following sensitive operator actions:
1) Install
2) Upgrade
3) Rollback
4) Config apply (including changes to audit export destinations, retention/residency, auth/RBAC bootstrap where applicable)
5) Credential rotation (installer-managed creds/certs/tokens)

Scope
- Private installer CLI + any invoked “apply”/“migrate” steps that mutate cluster state.
- Events must conform to Canonical Audit Event Schema v1 (incl. schema_version tagging) and use registered event_type values from the sensitive-actions taxonomy.
- Must work in air-gapped/offline mode (spool locally and forward when audit pipeline available).

Non-goals
- Building a full audit log UI for Private.
- Emitting fine-grained per-resource events for every Kubernetes object touched (keep cardinality controlled; emit summarized diffs).

Proposed event types (from taxonomy; names to be finalized in ENG-4822 registry)
- private.install.start / private.install.complete / private.install.failed
- private.upgrade.start / private.upgrade.complete / private.upgrade.failed
- private.rollback.start / private.rollback.complete / private.rollback.failed
- private.config.apply.start / private.config.apply.complete / private.config.apply.failed
- private.credentials.rotate.start / private.credentials.rotate.complete / private.credentials.rotate.failed

Required fields (schema v1)
- schema_version: "1.0"
- event_id: ULID preferred for time-sort + uniqueness (align with eng-security guidance on SIEM dedupe)
- event_type: from registry
- action: "start" | "complete" | "failed" (if encoded separately from event_type, follow registry convention)
- timestamp: RFC3339
- tenant_id / org_id: For Private, use private_installation_id + customer_org_external_id if present; otherwise emit installation_id and leave tenant_id blank per schema guidance (avoid inventing IDs).
- deployment_mode: "private"
- environment: "prod" | "staging" (from installer config)
- actor.*:
  - actor.type: "human" | "service" | "system"
  - actor.id: best-effort; if invoked by a human on a bastion, capture operator identifier via flags/env (see Operator identity section)
  - actor.display_name: optional
- source.*:
  - source.ip: best-effort (if known)
  - source.user_agent: installer version + command
  - source.host: hostname
- target.*:
  - target.type: "private_cluster" | "private_installation" | "audit_export_destination" | "credential" | "config"
  - target.id: cluster_id/installation_id (stable)
- correlation:
  - request_id: installer-run ID (one per command invocation)
  - trace_id: if OpenTelemetry trace exists; otherwise omit
  - admin_action_id: same as request_id for CLI actions (per ENG-4830 guidance)
- outcome:
  - outcome: "success" | "failure" | "unknown"
  - error_code/error_message: capped + normalized; no raw stack traces
- change_summary:
  - For config apply, include a bounded diff summary (keys changed + old/new redacted as required)

Operator identity (Private nuance)
We cannot rely on Redwood Console auth context. Proposal:
- Add optional installer flags/env:
  - --operator-email (or --operator-id) (validated format, stored as actor.id)
  - --change-ticket (Jira/ServiceNow) (stored as actor.session or metadata; capped)
- If not provided, actor.type="system" and actor.id="unknown"; still emit events.
- Do NOT capture shell username by default (PII + unreliability). Only capture when explicitly provided.

Redaction / secrets
- Never log:
  - kubeconfig contents, tokens, private keys, cert PEM bodies, API keys, registry auth
- For credential rotation events:
  - include credential_id or credential_type (e.g., "tls_cert", "service_token")
  - include fingerprint/hash (SHA-256) of cert/public material only if approved by Security (ENG-4832)

Delivery semantics
- Installer should not hard-fail core operations due to inability to deliver audit events (best-effort with guaranteed local persistence):
  - Always write audit events to a local spool file (encrypted-at-rest if feasible; otherwise ensure file permissions + documented risk)
  - Attempt delivery to audit ingestion endpoint when configured
  - On delivery failure, retry with backoff; expose status at end of command and exit code should reflect the underlying operation result, not audit sink failure
- Include event_id for downstream dedupe (customers seeing duplicates due to retry semantics; aligns with SUP-18510 discussion)

Acceptance criteria
1) For each command: install/upgrade/rollback/config apply/credential rotate, we emit start + complete/failed events.
2) Events validate against schema v1 and use only registered event_type values.
3) Events include request_id/admin_action_id and installation_id/cluster_id.
4) Config apply event includes bounded change_summary (no secrets; keys are allow-listed; unknown keys bucketed as "other" with count only).
5) Unit tests cover event emission for each command path and a failure path.
6) Integration/E2E: included in ENG-4834 harness for Private fixture (run at least nightly; PR-gated where feasible).
7) Documentation: Private operator doc snippet on how to set operator identity + where audit events are delivered/spooled.

Implementation plan
- Add shared audit event emitter to installer repo (consume shared schema library/validator where possible; otherwise embed JSON schema v1 for validation at build-time).
- Define installer "run context": request_id (ULID), operator info, host info, installer version, command.
- Wrap major state transitions with emitAuditEvent("*.start"), defer emitAuditEvent("*.complete") / catch emitAuditEvent("*.failed").
- Config diff:
  - Implement key allow-list: audit_export.*, retention.*, residency.*, auth.*, rbac_bootstrap.*
  - For sensitive keys: output "[REDACTED]"; for non-sensitive, output old/new capped to 256 chars.
- Spool:
  - Write newline-delimited JSON to /var/lib/redwood/audit-spool/installer.ndjson (configurable)
  - Rotation policy (size/time) + documented location

Risks / open questions
- Identity: operator flags are optional; do we require them in enterprise mode? (PM/Vivek leaning “recommended, not required”)
- Offline encryption: likely follow-up; for now rely on file perms + “do not store secrets” guarantee.
- Taxonomy: final event_type strings must match ENG-4822 registry; don’t merge until registry is finalized.

References / related
- Drive: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/private-deployments/private-audit-logging-parity-checklist.json
- Customer: SUP-18421 (Private missing audit events parity concern)
- Exporter/normalization: ENG-4827 (schema_version tagging) + SUP-18477 (inconsistent fields)
- Dedupe semantics: SUP-18510 (duplicates due to retries)

Planned PR
- Repo: redwood-private-installer
- Target PR: sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-611-emit-audit-events-during-install-upgrade-rollback.json (update/extend to include config apply + credential rotation + schema validation)

title: Telemetry coverage dashboard for sensitive-action audit events (expected vs observed + alerts)
preview:
Goal
Build an internal coverage dashboard and alerting that verifies we are emitting audit events for 100% of “sensitive actions” across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. Dashboard must show expected event types vs observed event types, broken down by producer service and deployment mode. Add automated alerts for regressions (missing or dropping events).

Why
- We need a repeatable, reviewable way to prove coverage (internal security/compliance + customer security reviews).
- We need regression detection: new endpoints/features frequently ship without audit emission.
- We need pipeline health separation from coverage (i.e., differentiate “service never emits” vs “sink is down”).

Scope (this ticket)
1) Coverage model + data source
- “Expected” set comes from the sensitive-actions event taxonomy registry (ENG-4822). Expected list includes:
  - event_type (canonical string)
  - required_dimensions: producer_service, deployment_mode, environment (prod/stage), schema_version
  - optional: tenant_scope applicability (hosted-only vs private-only)
- “Observed” set is computed from audit event telemetry ingested via observability pipeline (post-normalization, tagged with schema_version).
- Primary output is a dashboard + alert rules; NOT building new event emission here.

2) Dashboards
- Main dashboard: “Audit Logging Coverage (Sensitive Actions)”
  - Overall coverage % (expected vs observed distinct event types) for last 24h / 7d
  - Coverage by producer_service (control-plane, api-gateway, console-backend, policy-engine, private-installer, audit-log-exporter)
  - Coverage by deployment_mode (hosted | dedicated | private)
  - Coverage by schema_version (v1.x)
  - Table of missing (expected - observed) with owner hints (service ownership mapping) + link to runbook
- Secondary dashboard: “Audit Event Volume & Cardinality”
  - events/sec by event_type, producer_service
  - top event_types by volume
  - high-cardinality field guardrails: unique actor_id count, unique resource_id count, etc. (sanity only; not a block)

3) Automated alerts
- Alert: Coverage regression (P0)
  - Fires when any expected event_type has 0 observations in prod over rolling window (default 6h) for a given deployment_mode where applicable.
  - Suppress/route appropriately during known maintenance windows (private upgrade windows) via annotation label.
- Alert: Producer silence (P0)
  - Fires when a producer_service has a sudden drop >90% in audit event volume over 15m compared to 24h baseline.
  - This is to catch “instrumentation broke” independent of “new event type missing.”
- Alert: Normalization/schema validation failures (P1)
  - Use exporter/ingestion metrics: schema_validation_failed_total and normalization_dropped_total (depends on ENG-4827).
- Routing:
  - eng-sre for pipeline-wide ingestion/sink issues
  - eng-platform for producer regressions (service owners)
  - eng-security for schema/taxonomy issues

Non-goals
- Not a customer-facing UI.
- Not a full audit pipeline SLO (tracked separately in ENG-4835 / slo-toolkit).
- Not defining taxonomy or schema in this ticket (ENG-4821/4822).

Implementation plan
A) Define the comparison mechanism
- Create a materialized “expected_event_types” dataset from the event_type registry (ENG-4822). Options:
  1) Export registry as a checked-in CSV/JSON artifact consumed by dashboards (preferred for determinism).
  2) Query registry from a service endpoint (avoid; creates runtime dependency).
- Add metadata fields to expected list (at minimum: event_type, producer_service_owner, applicability: hosted/dedicated/private).

B) Observed dataset
- Use normalized audit events (post-exporter normalization) as the observed source of truth.
- Ensure we include dimensions:
  - event_type
  - producer_service
  - deployment_mode
  - environment
  - schema_version
  - timestamp
- Ensure sampling is not hiding gaps: observed metric should be built from raw event stream counts, not sampled traces.

C) Coverage computation
- Coverage per slice = count(distinct expected event_types) where observed_count>0 / total expected for that slice.
- Missing list = expected LEFT JOIN observed WHERE observed_count=0 over last N hours.
- Window defaults:
  - “Regression detection” window: 6h (prod)
  - “Slow/rare actions” window: 7d (dashboard only)
- For low-frequency actions (e.g., private install/upgrade), add an explicit “rare=true” flag in expected list; alerts should use 7d or be disabled in prod by default and monitored via weekly review.

D) Delivery
- Grafana dashboard in the standard on-call folder + link from:
  - Audit logging standard doc (Confluence)
  - SRE runbook: audit log coverage regression
  - Linear parent initiative
- Alert rules managed in observability-pack with code review.

Acceptance criteria
- Dashboard exists and is accessible to Security/GRC + relevant Eng teams.
- Dashboard correctly shows, for prod:
  - expected event types from registry
  - observed counts from normalized events
  - missing list with producer_service breakdown
- Coverage regression alert triggers in a controlled test (disable emission for a test event_type in staging) and routes to the right channel.
- Documentation includes:
  - definition of “observed” windowing
  - how to handle rare events (private installs/upgrades)
  - how to request an exception (waiver) without making the dashboard lie

Open questions
- “Rare actions” policy: do we want a separate alert channel for rare=true, or keep it dashboard-only and reviewed weekly by Security Eng?
- Multi-region: should coverage be computed per region, or roll-up across regions? (Initial proposal: roll-up across regions; add region drill-down later if needed.)

Notes / links
- Related tickets: ENG-4821 (schema), ENG-4822 (taxonomy registry + lints), ENG-4830 (correlation IDs), ENG-4827 (exporter normalization + schema tagging), ENG-4835 (drop-rate alerts), ENG-4834 (E2E tests asserting events).
- Likely code location for dashboards/alerts: observability-pack (see PR-433 for pipeline dashboards as reference).

Progress updates
- 2026-01-23 (Logan): Drafted dashboard wireframe + query plan; confirmed we can join expected registry artifact against observed events in ClickHouse without cross-cluster queries.
- 2026-02-04 (Nadia): Confirmed schema_version and producer_service are present post-normalization; missing deployment_mode for some legacy producers—tracked as follow-up in ENG-4833.
- 2026-02-14 (Allison): Pushed back on per-region alerts initially due to alert fatigue; agreed on roll-up + drill-down panels.
- 2026-02-21 (Sean): Requested separate alerts for “pipeline down” vs “coverage missing.” We’ll use ingestion lag/drop metrics for pipeline, and only fire coverage alerts when pipeline health is green.

Next steps
- Finalize expected registry export format with Sahana (CI) and Marcus (Security Eng) so dashboards stay in sync with taxonomy changes.
- Implement alerts in observability-pack and test end-to-end in staging.
- Add runbook snippets for interpreting the dashboard: when missing events are caused by (a) new endpoints, (b) exporter normalization drops, (c) pipeline lag.

title: Migrate legacy audit events to v1 normalized schema (schema_version tagging + field normalization)
preview:
Context
- We have multiple legacy audit event producers across API Gateway, Control Plane, Console backend, and Private installer that emit inconsistent payload shapes and field names.
- Customers consuming via SIEM export (Splunk/Sentinel/etc.) are seeing mismatched fields across event types and releases, and duplicates are harder to dedupe without stable event identifiers.
- This ticket is the migration plan + implementation coordination to ensure all legacy producers are normalized into the canonical v1 schema, with explicit schema_version tagging and a deprecation window for legacy fields.

Goals (what “done” means)
1) All audit events exported externally (and stored internally) conform to Audit Schema v1 OR are normalized to v1 at the exporter boundary.
2) Every exported event includes schema_version (e.g., "1.0") and stable event_id.
3) Legacy fields are either:
   - mapped to v1 fields, OR
   - retained temporarily as namespaced legacy.* extensions (explicitly documented), OR
   - dropped (with explicit deprecation notice + evidence it is safe).
4) A documented backcompat plan exists for customers, including de-dupe guidance (event_id) and field mapping notes.

Non-goals
- Not redesigning the taxonomy: event_type naming is handled by ENG-4822.
- Not building a customer-facing audit log UI beyond basic viewing/export configuration.

Scope
A) Identify legacy producers and payload variants
- Inventory producers that emit audit-like events to the pipeline today, including “security_event”, “admin_event”, and service-specific variants.
- For each producer: capture sample payload(s), transport path, and which customers are receiving them (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).

B) Implement normalization
Two-step strategy (preferred):
1) Exporter-side normalization (fastest, reduces customer pain)
   - audit-log-exporter validates payload against v1.
   - If schema_version missing or payload does not match v1, apply deterministic mapping to v1.
   - Tag normalized_from = "legacy" (namespaced extension) and record normalization outcome in exporter metrics.
2) Producer-side migration (longer-term correctness)
   - Update services to emit v1 directly using the shared schema library.
   - Remove legacy emission paths once parity verified.

C) Deprecate legacy fields
- Define a 2-release deprecation window for any legacy.* extensions that remain after normalization.
- Provide a customer-facing “field migration note” snippet for Support/Solutions that enumerates what changed and how to update SIEM parsing.

Known legacy patterns we must normalize (initial list; expand during inventory)
- Actor identity fields:
  - legacy: user_id, admin_email, principal, subject
  - v1: actor.type, actor.id, actor.display, actor.org_id (where applicable), actor.impersonated_by.*
- Request correlation:
  - legacy: requestId, trace, correlationId
  - v1: request_id, trace_id, admin_action_id
- Target/resource:
  - legacy: resource, resourceId, object_type, object_id
  - v1: target.type, target.id, target.display, target.org_id/tenant_id/project_id
- Action naming:
  - legacy: action (free-form), eventName
  - v1: event_type (from registry) + action.result
- Source info:
  - legacy: ip, client_ip, remoteAddr
  - v1: source_ip, user_agent (bounded), source.service
- Timestamps:
  - legacy: time (ms), createdAt (string)
  - v1: occurred_at (RFC3339), received_at (exporter-set)
- Error/result:
  - legacy: success boolean, error string
  - v1: action.outcome = success|failure, action.error_code, action.error_message (redacted)

Deliverables
- “Legacy → v1 mapping matrix” (kept in this ticket description + linked artifact once finalized).
- Exporter normalization implementation:
  - When receiving an event:
    - If schema_version present and valid: pass-through.
    - Else normalize to v1 and set schema_version = "1.0".
    - If cannot normalize: route to dead-letter with reason and emit metric; do NOT silently drop.
  - Metrics:
    - audit_exporter.normalization.applied_count{producer, legacy_shape}
    - audit_exporter.normalization.failed_count{producer, reason}
    - audit_exporter.schema_validation.failed_count{producer}
- Producer migration plan:
  - List of producers and target PRs/tickets to update them to emit v1 directly.
- Deprecation + comms:
  - Internal Support FAQ snippet and customer guidance notes for SIEM parsing.

Acceptance criteria
- For a representative set of legacy payloads from each producer (at least 3 per producer), exporter emits v1-compliant events that pass JSON Schema validation.
- SIEM export samples for Splunk and Sentinel show consistent top-level fields across event types, including schema_version and event_id.
- No secrets/credentials are ever logged during normalization (confirm redaction patterns apply).
- Coverage dashboard (ENG-4828) shows no “unknown legacy shape” events > 0.1% for 7 days after rollout (or documented exceptions with owners).

Rollout plan
1) Inventory + samples (week 1)
   - Gather raw samples from:
     - Control plane (RBAC + API key lifecycle)
     - API gateway admin endpoints
     - Console admin workflows
     - Private installer upgrade/config flows
   - Confirm which are already v1 vs legacy.
2) Implement exporter normalization + metrics (week 1–2)
   - Land behind feature flag: AUDIT_NORMALIZE_LEGACY_V1=true
   - Canary enable for internal tenants + one low-risk Dedicated customer.
3) Broaden enablement (week 2–3)
   - Enable for all Hosted/Dedicated.
   - Enable for Private packages >= private-installer 0.19.x (coordinate with Vivek).
4) Producer-side migrations (parallel; week 2–6)
   - Convert producers to shared schema library to reduce reliance on exporter transforms.
5) Deprecate legacy.* extensions (post 2 releases)
   - Remove legacy fields from exports after notice window; keep exporter able to normalize older producers for Private customers lagging on upgrades.

Risks / open questions
- Idempotency/deduplication: exporter retries can generate duplicates downstream. Ensure event_id is stable and recommend SIEM de-dupe on event_id + occurred_at. (See SUP-18510.)
- Private upgrades: customers may run older installer versions for extended periods; exporter must remain tolerant of legacy producers longer for Private.
- Cardinality: legacy free-form “action” strings must be mapped to enum event_type; unknown actions should go to a controlled "unknown" bucket with alerting, not create new event types.

References
- Customer pain:
  - SUP-18477 (SIEM export fields not normalized)
  - SUP-18510 (duplicates due to retry semantics)
- Related implementation work:
  - ENG-4821 (schema v1 definition)
  - ENG-4822 (event taxonomy + lints)
  - ENG-4827 (exporter normalization + schema_version tagging)
  - ENG-4830 (correlation IDs)
  - ENG-4832 (redaction rules)
- PRs:
  - github/audit-log-exporter/pr-842 (schema_version tagging and normalization) – this ticket tracks rollout + completeness, not just the PR merge

Owner notes
- Primary owner: Michael Nguyen (backcompat/migrations)
- Exporter implementation partner: Kevin Osei
- Security review: Hanae Suzuki (redaction + field classification)
- Private parity coordination: Vivek Kulkarni / Amina Yusuf

title: Security review follow-ups: implement mitigations from offline bundle signing threat model
preview:
Context
- This ticket tracks execution of required mitigations identified in the threat model for Offline Bundle Signing.
- Threat model doc: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/threat-model-offline-bundle-signing.json
- Design/ADR references:
  - sources/confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/offline-signing-and-verification-design.json
  - sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0147-offline-artifact-signing-scheme.json

Scope (what this ticket is responsible for)
- Close out the threat model action items that are prerequisites for pilot/GA.
- Ensure actions are implemented in code and/or documented in a way that passes security review and customer scrutiny (air-gapped / regulated).
- Produce clear evidence hooks: audit logs, change control, approvals, and key ceremony artifacts.

Out of scope
- Customer-signed bundles / customer trust roots beyond documenting a future plan.
- Broader packaging overhaul unrelated to signing/verification.

Threat model actions to close (owners, implementation plan, acceptance criteria)

A) Signing key isolation + custody (Owner: Hanae Suzuki, Support: Connor O'Brien)
1) Key storage
   - Requirement: signing private key must never be exportable from protected boundary; prefer HSM-backed KMS where possible.
   - Implementation notes:
     - CI signing step uses a dedicated signing service identity (non-human), backed by KMS/HSM key.
     - No long-lived private key material in CI runners.
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Evidence of non-exportable key configuration (screenshot/attestation in key ceremony packet).
     - CI job uses KMS Sign operation only; no raw key access paths.

2) Access control + separation of duties
   - Requirement: limit who can initiate signing and who can approve releases.
   - Implementation notes:
     - RBAC policy for signing operator role is least privilege (ties to rbac-policy-bundle PR-9441).
     - Release cut requires 2-person approval (Release Eng + Security delegate) for key use policy changes and rotation.
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Documented approval matrix in key ceremony/evidence guide.
     - Access review checklist included and followed for any new signing operator.

B) Build/release provenance + reproducibility signals (Owner: Sahana Iyer, Support: Devon Price)
1) Provenance capture
   - Requirement: produce machine-readable provenance for the signed bundle build (inputs, tool versions, build ID) that can be retained in Redwood evidence stores.
   - Implementation notes:
     - Signed manifest includes build_id / commit refs where feasible.
     - Release pipeline stores immutable build logs and artifact hashes.
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Bundle contains signed manifest with build_id and artifact hashes.
     - CI retains logs and signature outputs for >= 1 year (or per policy) for Private releases.

2) Deterministic manifest canonicalization
   - Requirement: prevent signature mismatches due to non-deterministic JSON/YAML serialization.
   - Implementation notes:
     - Canonical JSON encoding rules finalized in ENG-4821.
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Two independent builds from same inputs produce identical canonical manifest bytes and signature verification passes.

C) Anti-tamper + anti-replay + downgrade prevention (Owner: Jordan Lee, Support: Ethan Park)
1) Tamper resistance
   - Requirement: installer must fail closed if any artifact listed in manifest is modified/swapped/truncated.
   - Implementation notes:
     - Integration tests cover modified manifest, swapped artifact, truncated bundle (ENG-4827).
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Preflight verification fails with deterministic error code and log trail.

2) Replay/downgrade protections
   - Requirement: prevent installing older vulnerable bundle when newer version already installed (unless explicitly using documented rollback procedure).
   - Implementation notes:
     - Signed manifest includes version metadata; installer stores last-installed version state (see PR-24902).
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Downgrade attempt is blocked by default with actionable message.
     - Explicit rollback path is documented and emits elevated audit events.

D) Verification UX, break-glass controls, and auditability (Owner: Logan Wright + Kevin Osei)
1) Audit event schema + local retention guidance
   - Requirement: emit local audit events for verify success/failure, including reason codes, bundle identifiers, and whether break-glass was used.
   - References:
     - sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/bundle-verification-audit-events.json
     - audit-log-exporter PR-3112
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Events are produced locally in Private environments with configurable redaction.
     - Docs specify recommended retention and export guidance (SIEM where available).

2) Break-glass (override) guardrails
   - Requirement: break-glass flag is policy-gated, noisy, and leaves an audit trail; requires documented approvals.
   - Implementation notes:
     - Installer supports break-glass override with explicit warnings and audit marker (PR-24979).
     - Org policy doc for exceptions:
       - sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions/exception-process-unsigned-bundle-hotfix.json
   - Acceptance criteria:
     - Break-glass path requires explicit operator intent and produces a distinct audit event.
     - Runbook includes approval steps + follow-up requirement to restore normal verification.

E) Time validity / air-gapped clock skew mitigation (Owner: Ethan Park)
- Requirement: avoid false negatives in air-gapped environments where NTP is restricted while maintaining security posture.
- Implementation notes:
  - PR-24961 introduces guidance + optional diagnostic time override (must be logged and not used for normal operation).
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Default behavior remains strict; diagnostic override is clearly labeled and emits audit event.
  - Support runbook includes time-skew triage steps.

Deliverables
- Code changes across installer/pipeline/logging as per dependencies.
- Updated security evidence package:
  - sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence/offline-bundle-signing-key-ceremony.json
- Updated operational runbooks:
  - sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/release-signing-key-rotation-runbook.json
  - sources/confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/runbook-offline-bundle-verification-failures.json

Status / progress notes
- 2026-01-08 (Marcus): Threat model review completed; flagged top GA blockers: key custody clarity, break-glass policy, audit event schema finalization.
- 2026-01-15 (Hanae): Drafted KMS/HSM-backed signing service proposal; requested least-privilege review for signing operator role (ties to internal access process).
- 2026-01-22 (Kevin): Proposed verification audit event fields + redaction rules; coordinating with audit-log-exporter updates.
- 2026-01-28 (Connor): Release train gating draft updated to require signed bundle + verification tests prior to cutting Private release artifacts (ENG-4862).

Open questions
1) Exact retention period for signing pipeline logs/evidence for Private releases (Security/GRC to confirm; default proposal: >= 12 months, align with existing release evidence retention).
2) Whether diagnostic time override should require separate flag + explicit acknowledgement string (Security prefers yes).

Risks
- Air-gapped customers may have strict crypto module requirements (FIPS) that impact key backend choice; ensure we can produce a clear statement of what’s used in the signing service and what’s required on customer side for verification.
- Break-glass overuse risk: need guardrails + support enforcement.

Go/No-Go criteria for Pilot (security)

title: Break-glass override: design + policy for offline bundle signature verification
preview:
Context
We are adding cryptographic signing + verification for Redwood Private offline bundles in air-gapped environments. By default we must fail closed when signature/manifest checks fail. However, pilots and regulated customers have asked for a documented “break-glass” path for emergency situations (e.g., time skew, trust store mismatch during key rotation, or urgent hotfix where verification cannot be performed normally).

This ticket defines:
1) Installer mechanics for break-glass override (what flags exist, what they do, what they do NOT do)
2) Guardrails/policy gating to prevent casual use
3) Audit logging requirements (local-first) + support bundle inclusion
4) Documentation + support runbooks + exception process alignment
5) Risk assessment and mitigations

Goals / Non-goals
Goals
- Provide a controlled override that is explicit, hard to invoke accidentally, and produces strong local audit evidence.
- Make the override behavior consistent across install and upgrade flows.
- Ensure customers can meet internal change control requirements (who approved, why used, what artifacts installed).

Non-goals (phase 1)
- Customer-signed bundles or customer-managed trust roots (tracked separately; see notes from eng-security thread 1760784422).
- Fully-online approval workflows (air-gapped). Any gating must work offline.

Proposed behavior (mechanics)
Default: installer preflight verifies bundle signature + manifest hashes + trust chain. Any failure blocks install/upgrade.

Break-glass mode: single explicit action that downgrades verification failure from “block” to “allow with loud warnings + mandatory audit markers”, but does not disable integrity checks silently.

Decision: implement as an installer flag + policy gate.
- Flag: --break-glass
- Additional required flags:
  - --break-glass-reason "<free text>" (required; min length 20; stored in local audit, not exported by default)
  - --break-glass-approval-id "<ticket/change-id>" (required; e.g., customer change request ID or Redwood support ticket)
  - --i-understand-this-weakens-supply-chain-security (required exact string) to reduce accidental invocation

Scope of what --break-glass allows:
- Allows install to proceed when:
  - signature is invalid OR
  - signing key is unknown/untrusted OR
  - signature validity window fails due to not-yet-valid/expired (time skew)
- Does NOT allow install to proceed when:
  - manifest hash mismatch vs bundle contents (tampering indicator)
  - manifest is missing required fields (format corruption)
  - artifact hashes fail (e.g., chart tarball digest mismatch)

Rationale: break-glass is meant for trust/validation issues (keys/time) but not for content integrity mismatch. If we allow hash mismatches, we eliminate our strongest offline tamper detection.

Policy gating / guardrails
We need an offline, customer-operable but policy-controlled mechanism.
- Introduce local policy file: /etc/redwood-private/installer-policy.yaml (path configurable via --policy-path)
- Break-glass only permitted when policy contains:
  - allow_break_glass: true
  - break_glass_requires_local_admin: true (enforced by requiring root/admin execution; installer already requires elevated privileges for install)
- Additionally, require a local “operator acknowledgement” file to exist (created by customer security team): /etc/redwood-private/break-glass-ack.txt
  - File content must include organization name + date + contact (free-form). Installer only checks existence + non-empty content; content is recorded as sha256 (not raw) in audit event to avoid leaking internal info.

Why not require a signed token from Redwood?
- Air-gapped environments often have no path to retrieve a fresh token during incidents.
- We can revisit for phase 2 as an optional “stronger gating” mode.

RBAC / separation of duties (Redwood side)
- For Redwood-run pilot support, document that Redwood personnel should not recommend break-glass unless (a) Support/Security approves per exception policy, and (b) customer records approval id.
- Align with RBAC policy bundle guardrails (see rbac-policy-bundle PR-9458) for internal operator role; even though on-prem is customer-run, our runbooks must reflect separation of duties.

Audit logging requirements
When break-glass is used, emit local audit events (and include in support bundle) with strong, consistent fields.
Event: bundle.verification.break_glass_used
Minimum fields (local audit event payload; exact schema owned by ENG-4829 but requirements are here):
- timestamp
- installer_version
- host_id (stable but non-PII; e.g., machine-id hash)
- effective_user (uid / username if available)
- bundle_build_id (from manifest)
- bundle_version (from manifest)
- bundle_path
- verification_failure_class (e.g., UNKNOWN_KEY, INVALID_SIGNATURE, CERT_NOT_YET_VALID, CERT_EXPIRED)
- break_glass_reason (as provided; stored locally; redaction config applies)
- break_glass_approval_id
- policy_path
- policy_hash
- ack_file_present: true/false
- ack_file_hash (sha256 of content) if present

Additionally:
- Emit bundle.verification.failed (normal) even if break-glass proceeds, but mark outcome=OVERRIDDEN.
- Installer output must print:
  - A clear banner: “BREAK-GLASS OVERRIDE ACTIVE — SUPPLY CHAIN VERIFICATION BYPASSED (PARTIAL)”.
  - The exact local log paths and event ids.
  - Next steps to return to safe mode: fix trust store/time, re-run verify, remove allow_break_glass policy.

Support bundle integration
- Include:
  - verification report (what failed)
  - whether break-glass was used
  - break_glass_approval_id
  - hashes for policy + ack (not raw contents)

Documentation deliverables
- Customer-facing guide (DevEx/docs) should include:
  - When break-glass is appropriate vs not appropriate
  - How to enable policy gate
  - Required flags and examples
  - Required local evidence to retain (change ticket, logs)
- Internal runbooks (Support/SE) should include:
  - Scripted decision tree: try trust-store update / time override diagnostic / verify-only mode first
  - Escalation to Security Eng for approval per exception process

Risk assessment
Primary risks introduced by break-glass:
1) Social/process risk: teams use break-glass as a convenience and normalize bypassing verification.
2) Malware/tampering risk: attacker convinces operator to use break-glass to install malicious bundle.
3) Forensics risk: insufficient local evidence to reconstruct what happened.

Mitigations
- Break-glass does not bypass hash mismatches; it is scoped to key/time/signature validation only.
- Policy-gated enablement; default is disabled.
- Friction to invoke (multi-flag acknowledgement, required reason + approval id).
- Loud local audit events; explicit warnings; support bundle inclusion.
- Documentation clearly stating that break-glass requires customer security approval.

Acceptance criteria
- Installer refuses --break-glass unless allow_break_glass is true in policy.
- --break-glass requires reason + approval id + acknowledgement phrase.
- Break-glass cannot proceed on manifest/artifact hash mismatch.
- All break-glass invocations emit audit event(s) and are included in support bundle output.
- Docs/runbooks updated and linked.
- Security Eng sign-off (Marcus) + GRC awareness (Olga) on wording + evidence expectations.

Implementation notes / PRs
- redwood-private-installer: add break-glass flag and audit warning (PR: sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-24979-add-break-glass-flag-and-audit-warning.json)
- rbac-policy-bundle: break-glass policy guardrails (PR: sources/github/rbac-policy-bundle/pr-9458-add-break-glass-policy-guardrails.json)
- Confluence exception process: unsigned bundle hotfix (refs: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions/exception-process-unsigned-bundle-hotfix.json)

Open questions (resolved in comments below)
- Should break-glass allow unknown key vs invalid signature? (Decision: allow both, but never allow hash mismatch.)
- How to handle time skew? (Decision: add diagnostic time override separately; break-glass can allow CERT_NOT_YET_VALID/EXPIRED but must log failure class.)
- Do we require a Redwood-provided token? (Decision: no for phase 1; revisit.)

title: Define audit events + export behavior for offline bundle signature verification outcomes
preview:
Problem
Air-gapped / offline Redwood Private installs must cryptographically verify offline bundles (manifest + signature). Customers (especially regulated) also require an auditable record of verification outcomes, with clear failure reasons and the ability to export to SIEM when available.

Today we have a mix of installer stdout/stderr logs and ad-hoc telemetry. There is no normalized event schema that:
- is consistent across success + failure paths
- carries sufficient provenance (bundle build id, key id, algorithm, manifest digest)
- supports redaction rules (no secrets, minimal PII)
- can be exported via audit-log-exporter for customers that forward logs
- is stable across versions (schema versioning)

Goal
Define:
1) The canonical audit event names for verification-related outcomes (success/failure/break-glass).
2) A minimal stable schema (fields + semantics) with explicit redaction guidance.
3) Where events are emitted (installer local audit log, optional cluster audit log sink).
4) Export behavior/mapping for customers using audit-log-exporter (format, fields, and defaults).

Non-goals (this ticket)
- Implementing the full verification flow in installer (tracked under ENG-4823 / related PRs)
- Designing customer-managed signing keys or multi-party signing
- Providing real-time upload of events to Redwood SaaS (Private deployments remain customer-controlled)

Proposed event taxonomy
We will standardize on the namespace: redwood.private.bundle_verification.*

Events (MVP)
A) redwood.private.bundle_verification.succeeded
Emitted when a bundle is verified (manifest canonicalization + signature + artifact hashes + optional SBOM pointer hash) and install proceeds.

B) redwood.private.bundle_verification.failed
Emitted when verification fails and install is blocked (fail-closed).

C) redwood.private.bundle_verification.override_used
Emitted when operator uses break-glass override (policy-gated) to proceed despite verification failure/unavailability.

D) redwood.private.bundle_verification.skipped
Emitted only for explicitly unsupported/legacy flows (e.g., pre-signing bundles) and only when user has set an explicit allow-legacy flag. (We should treat this as temporary; see Rollout notes.)

Note: We explicitly do NOT emit per-file hash verification events to avoid log volume and leaking filenames. We include aggregate counts + top-level digests.

Schema (v1)
All events share these common fields:
- schema_version: "1"
- event_name: one of the above
- event_time: RFC3339 (installer local time; see Time Skew notes below)
- severity: INFO|WARN|ERROR (succeeded=INFO, failed=ERROR, override_used=WARN, skipped=WARN)
- component: "redwood-private-installer"
- component_version: installer semver (e.g., 2.7.0)
- host_id: stable local identifier (machine id hash) if available; otherwise omitted
- actor: local username or service account name if available; otherwise "unknown" (redaction rules apply)
- request_id: generated per invocation / install session (uuid)
- install_session_id: stable across multiple steps of same run (uuid)

Verification-specific fields:
- bundle_path: string (LOCAL ONLY by default; do not export unless customer opts in; see redaction)
- bundle_build_id: string (from manifest, e.g., "private-2026.01.0+build.18492")
- bundle_version: string (human release version, e.g., "2026.01.0")
- manifest_digest: sha256 of canonical manifest bytes
- signature_digest: sha256 of signature bytes (detached signature file)
- signing_key_id: stable key identifier (e.g., "redwood-release-key-2025q4")
- signing_key_fingerprint: public key fingerprint (sha256) (safe to export)
- signature_algorithm: e.g., "ed25519" or "ecdsa-p256" (per ADR)
- trust_store_id: identifier for which trust store set was used (e.g., "embedded-v2" or "customer-updated-2026-01")
- trust_store_version: semver or date string

Outcome fields:
- outcome: "success"|"failure"|"override"|"skipped"
- failure_code: enum string when outcome=failure (see Failure taxonomy)
- failure_detail: short human-readable detail (single line; no stack traces; no file paths unless local-only)
- remediation_hint: short hint suitable for support docs (e.g., "Verify bundle transfer integrity and rerun bundle inspect")

Aggregate verification metrics:
- artifact_count_total: integer (as string in log JSON)
- artifact_count_verified: integer
- artifact_count_failed: integer
- sbom_present: "true"|"false"

Downgrade/replay signals:
- install_state_present: "true"|"false"
- installed_bundle_build_id: previous installed build id (if present)
- downgrade_detected: "true"|"false"

Break-glass fields (override_used only)
- override_flag: the CLI flag used (e.g., "--break-glass")
- override_reason: operator-supplied reason (required)
- override_ticket: external ticket reference (e.g., "SUP-31977")
- override_approver: name/email if operator inputs it (optional)

Failure taxonomy (failure_code)
We want stable codes for support triage and SIEM detection rules. Proposed list:
- MANIFEST_MISSING
- MANIFEST_PARSE_ERROR
- MANIFEST_CANONICALIZATION_ERROR
- MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH
- SIGNATURE_MISSING
- SIGNATURE_INVALID
- SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED
- SIGNING_KEY_REVOKED
- SIGNATURE_EXPIRED
- SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID
- TIME_SOURCE_UNRELIABLE
- ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH
- ARTIFACT_MISSING
- SBOM_POINTER_MISSING
- SBOM_HASH_MISMATCH
- DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED
- INTERNAL_ERROR

Redaction + privacy guidance
- Do not include raw file lists, image names, registry URLs, chart names, or model names in exported audit events (can be sensitive in regulated environments).
- bundle_path is considered sensitive metadata and should be local-only by default. Exporter should omit unless customer explicitly enables "include_paths".
- actor may be considered PII. Exporter should support hashing actor (sha256) by default. Keep raw actor only in local logs unless customer opts into exporting raw actor.
- failure_detail must be single-line, sanitized, and must not include stack traces or internal hostnames.

Event emission locations
MVP emission targets:
1) Local installer audit log (jsonl): /var/log/redwood/audit.log (Linux default) and Windows equivalent.
2) If audit-log-exporter is installed: exporter reads local audit.log and forwards to customer SIEM.

We will also wire a subset to Redwood Private cluster audit events (if cluster is running) but MUST NOT require cluster reachability for on-prem install preflight.

Export behavior (audit-log-exporter mapping)
- Export format: JSON object per event; preserve schema_version + event_name; include a top-level "category": "bundle_verification".
- Default included fields: event_time, severity, component, component_version, request_id, install_session_id, bundle_build_id, bundle_version, manifest_digest, signature_digest, signing_key_fingerprint, signature_algorithm, trust_store_id, trust_store_version, outcome, failure_code, remediation_hint, downgrade_detected.
- Default excluded fields: bundle_path, raw actor, override_approver (unless configured).
- Provide configuration knobs in exporter:
  - include_paths: false by default
  - actor_mode: "hash" (default) | "raw" | "omit"
  - include_override_fields: true (override_reason is required; but may be sensitive—customer choice)

Time skew handling
Air-gapped environments frequently have skewed clocks. We will:
- Always include event_time (local) but also include optional monotonic sequence number within the session: event_seq (integer as string)
- When signature validity checks fail due to time (NOT_YET_VALID / EXPIRED), set failure_code accordingly and include a specific remediation_hint pointing to time sync guidance.
- Avoid claiming absolute accuracy of event_time in compliance language; treat it as "reported local time".

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (Pilot): emit events locally; exporter mapping behind feature flag "bundle_verification_events".
- Phase 2 (Limited): enable exporter mapping by default; docs update with example SIEM queries.
- Phase 3 (GA): deprecate "skipped" events for legacy unsigned bundles; require signed bundles except for documented exception process.

Acceptance criteria
- A written schema v1 is agreed by Security Eng + GRC and published in Confluence (bundle-verification-audit-events page).
- Installer implementation emits succeeded/failed/override_used events with stable failure_code values.
- audit-log-exporter mapping ships with safe defaults (paths excluded, actor hashed).
- Support runbook references failure_code list and maps to remediation steps.
- Integration tests include at least: invalid signature, unknown key, hash mismatch, not-yet-valid, downgrade blocked, and break-glass used; each must produce one audit event with expected fields.

Open questions
- Do we need a separate event for "verification attempted" to help measure incomplete installs? (Concern: noise; likely not for MVP.)
- How do we represent key revocation in air-gapped trust store updates (revocation list vs trust store version bump)? (May become schema v2.)
- Should we emit events to Kubernetes audit logs once cluster is running, or keep this entirely installer-side? (Preference: installer-side for completeness.)

References
- Confluence: security-and-compliance/audit-logging/bundle-verification-audit-events
- GitHub PRs (in flight): redwood-private-installer PR-24947 (telemetry), audit-log-exporter PR-3112 (schema), redwood PR-56247 (audit event emission path)
- Related threads: eng-security audit event schema review; support FAQ compilation for offline errors

Owner / reviewers
- Owner: Kevin Osei
- Eng partner: Logan Wright (installer telemetry hooks)
- Standards: Allison Grant (logging/audit standards)
- Security review: Marcus Lin
- GRC evidence mapping: Olga Petrov / Daniel Carter

title: Vendor Gateway: pricing-bundles experiment + SOC2 evidence handshake
preview: Create a vendor-facing onboarding gateway that couples a set of pricing/packaging experiments (bundle configurations and insurance crediting) with an automated SOC2 evidence handshake to reduce procurement friction for enterprise customers. The goal is to enable self-serve procurement for second-tier enterprise deals while surfacing needed legal/finance guardrails and collecting verifiable evidence for SOC2/insurance without repeated manual requests.

title: Quickstart landing — interactive hero with architecture strip and updated screenshot set
preview: Objective: Replace the static quickstart hero with an interactive hero that introduces the product in two layers: (1) a clean marketing-facing headline + CTA for new users and (2) an expandable architecture strip that explains the request flow (client → routing/policy → model variant → KV cache → output) for engineering audiences. Scope: hero composition, supporting 3 responsive breakpoints (desktop/tablet/mobile), an 'architecture strip' micro-interaction, and an updated set of product screenshots (hosted API request, dedicated capacity dashboard snapshot, private VPC connection modal). Constraints: minimal engineering effort for landing page rollout (we must limit new frontend components to an expandable strip + animated microtransitions), maintain <3s LCP for hero, ensure WCAG AA contrast and keyboard/ screen-reader accessibility for the interactive strip. Trade-offs considered: full interactive simulator would provide the best learning signal but increases implementation time and testing surface; instead we are shipping a progressive disclosure strip with layered content and sample token-cost numbers. Deliverables: Figma hero comps (3 breakpoints), annotated design tokens, screenshot checklist & capture recipe, final PNG/WEBP assets exported + accessibility alt copy, rollout A/B test variants (control = current hero, variant A = interactive strip collapsed, variant B = interactive strip expanded by default).

title: Security review: log redaction + retention defaults for Private observability minimum pack
preview:
Context
- This ticket is the required Security & Compliance review for the Redwood Private “observability minimum pack” logging portion.
- Goal: ensure (1) Redwood components do not emit secrets/PII into operational logs by default, (2) the default retention guidance/values shipped in Helm/installer are compliant and defensible, and (3) export integrations (Splunk HEC / Elastic/OpenSearch / Loki / OTLP where applicable) include safe defaults and clear customer responsibility boundaries.

In-scope components/log sources
- Control plane: API gateway, auth/RBAC integration points, policy/routing service, installer/upgrade controller, model registry integration service.
- Data plane: serving runtime pods, request router, batching/caching components.
- Observability plumbing: log collector config shipped in the minimum pack (baseline collector + parsing/filters), Helm values defaults.

Threat model / what we must prevent
- Secrets: bearer tokens, API keys, OAuth tokens, session cookies, K8s service account tokens, KMS material, private keys, HEC tokens, basic auth creds, database connection strings.
- PII: raw end-user identifiers, emails, names, IP addresses (where treated as personal data), prompt/completion text that may contain customer data.
- Sensitive customer data: request/response bodies, tool arguments, system prompts, retrieval content, model inputs/outputs.

Proposed “safe by default” logging policy (minimum pack)
1) No payload logging by default
- Do not log prompt text, completion text, tool arguments, or request/response bodies in any Redwood Private component at INFO/WARN/ERROR.
- If payload logging is needed for debugging, require explicit opt-in (feature flag + documented risk), with automatic sampling + max-length truncation.

2) Allowed fields (operational logs)
- request_id (opaque UUID)
- trace_id/span_id (if present)
- component/service name, pod/node, version, cluster_id (customer-defined)
- route (bounded cardinality; do not include raw URL path params)
- model (bounded; use Redwood model id, not customer prompt)
- tenant_id: store as irreversible hash only (no raw tenant/org names)
- counts/metrics: token counts, queue depth, latency, batch size, cache hit/miss, GPU id (bounded)

3) Denylist + redaction rules (must be applied centrally in collector AND at source when feasible)
- Header keys: Authorization, Proxy-Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie, X-Api-Key, X-Amz-Security-Token
- Common JSON keys: api_key, token, access_token, refresh_token, client_secret, password, passwd, secret, private_key, session
- Connection strings: redact inline credentials in URLs (e.g., postgres://user:pass@…)
- Splunk HEC: never log HEC token; treat as secret in values + config templates

4) IP address handling
- Default: do not log client_ip. If required for abuse/forensics, support a toggle to log a truncated /24 (IPv4) or /56 (IPv6) prefix, clearly documented as personal data.

5) Error logging hygiene
- Stack traces are allowed but must not include raw request context.
- gRPC/HTTP error messages must not echo payload; ensure middleware does not include request body in error output.

Retention defaults and compliance notes
- Redwood Private deployments are customer-controlled; however we ship defaults in charts and we document “recommended supported minimum.”
- Proposed defaults for the minimum pack:
  - Operational logs (collector -> sink): 7 days retention by default (where we ship an in-cluster sink such as Loki); recommend 7–14 days typical; warn against >30 days without compliance review.
  - Metrics (Prometheus): 15 days retention default (storage permitting); not generally considered PII but may contain identifiers if mis-labeled.
  - Audit logs: not part of the operational log pipeline; must follow the customer’s compliance policy. Document recommended minimum of 90 days where required by policy, with strict separation from operational logs.
- Explicitly document customer responsibilities:
  - Choosing retention period based on regulatory requirements (GDPR/CCPA, SOC2 evidence needs, internal policy)
  - Setting access controls (RBAC) on log systems and export destinations
  - Configuring encryption at rest/in transit for their sinks

Review artifacts / references
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-022-log-pipeline-defaults-private.json
- Security/audit interactions doc: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/private-observability-and-audit-log-interactions.json
- Slack thread (redaction policy): sources/slack/eng-security/1746556902-log-redaction-policy-for-private.json
- Implementation PR (structured fields + redaction safeguards): sources/github/redwood/pr-18521-add-structured-logging-fields-for-private-troubleshooting.json
- Export support work: ENG-19150 (Splunk HEC), ENG-19151 (Elastic/OpenSearch), ENG-19149 (collector defaults)

Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Confirm component-level logging does not emit payload text or secrets at INFO/WARN/ERROR under default config.
- [ ] Verify denylist redaction is applied in collector config templates shipped with the minimum pack.
- [ ] Verify Helm values do not render secrets into ConfigMaps (must be Secrets) and that logs do not print rendered configs on startup.
- [ ] Retention defaults documented and included in values (with clear customer override guidance).
- [ ] Add a lightweight automated “log scrub” test to smoke test harness (fail build on matched secret patterns).
- [ ] Sign-off from Security Engineering (Marcus Lin) + Compliance/Product Security (Naomi Feldman / Kevin Osei as needed).

Open questions
- Do we support OTLP log export in v1 minimum pack, or only document it as “experimental” (per prior debate)? If OTLP logs are supported, ensure metadata filtering aligns with the policy above.
- Should we ship client_ip logging toggle at all in v1, or defer to premium/compliance pack?

Proposed next steps
- Security review meeting: review current log samples from staging private cluster; validate against policy.
- Patch any violations upstream (middleware error logging, request logging interceptors).
- Update docs with explicit do/don’t list for customers and a short incident response note: “How to capture payloads safely if absolutely required.”
