- Control plane: API gateway, auth/RBAC integration points, policy/routing service, installer/upgrade controller, model registry integration service.
- Data plane: serving runtime pods, request router, batching/caching components.
- Observability plumbing: log collector config shipped in the minimum pack (baseline collector + parsing/filters), Helm values defaults.

Threat model / what we must prevent
- Secrets: bearer tokens, API keys, OAuth tokens, session cookies, K8s service account tokens, KMS material, private keys, HEC tokens, basic auth creds, database connection strings.
- PII: raw end-user identifiers, emails, names, IP addresses (where treated as personal data), prompt/completion text that may contain customer data.
- Sensitive customer data: request/response bodies, tool arguments, system prompts, retrieval content, model inputs/outputs.

Proposed “safe by default” logging policy (minimum pack)
1) No payload logging by default
- Do not log prompt text, completion text, tool arguments, or request/response bodies in any Redwood Private component at INFO/WARN/ERROR.
- If payload logging is needed for debugging, require explicit opt-in (feature flag + documented risk), with automatic sampling + max-length truncation.

2) Allowed fields (operational logs)
- request_id (opaque UUID)
- trace_id/span_id (if present)
- component/service name, pod/node, version, cluster_id (customer-defined)
- route (bounded cardinality; do not include raw URL path params)
- model (bounded; use Redwood model id, not customer prompt)
- tenant_id: store as irreversible hash only (no raw tenant/org names)
- counts/metrics: token counts, queue depth, latency, batch size, cache hit/miss, GPU id (bounded)

3) Denylist + redaction rules (must be applied centrally in collector AND at source when feasible)
- Header keys: Authorization, Proxy-Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie, X-Api-Key, X-Amz-Security-Token
- Common JSON keys: api_key, token, access_token, refresh_token, client_secret, password, passwd, secret, private_key, session
- Connection strings: redact inline credentials in URLs (e.g., postgres://user:pass@…)
- Splunk HEC: never log HEC token; treat as secret in values + config templates

4) IP address handling
- Default: do not log client_ip. If required for abuse/forensics, support a toggle to log a truncated /24 (IPv4) or /56 (IPv6) prefix, clearly documented as personal data.

5) Error logging hygiene
- Stack traces are allowed but must not include raw request context.
- gRPC/HTTP error messages must not echo payload; ensure middleware does not include request body in error output.

Retention defaults and compliance notes
- Redwood Private deployments are customer-controlled; however we ship defaults in charts and we document “recommended supported minimum.”
- Proposed defaults for the minimum pack:
  - Operational logs (collector -> sink): 7 days retention by default (where we ship an in-cluster sink such as Loki); recommend 7–14 days typical; warn against >30 days without compliance review.
  - Metrics (Prometheus): 15 days retention default (storage permitting); not generally considered PII but may contain identifiers if mis-labeled.
  - Audit logs: not part of the operational log pipeline; must follow the customer’s compliance policy. Document recommended minimum of 90 days where required by policy, with strict separation from operational logs.
- Explicitly document customer responsibilities:
  - Choosing retention period based on regulatory requirements (GDPR/CCPA, SOC2 evidence needs, internal policy)
  - Setting access controls (RBAC) on log systems and export destinations
  - Configuring encryption at rest/in transit for their sinks

Review artifacts / references
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-022-log-pipeline-defaults-private.json
- Security/audit interactions doc: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/private-observability-and-audit-log-interactions.json
- Slack thread (redaction policy): sources/slack/eng-security/1746556902-log-redaction-policy-for-private.json
- Implementation PR (structured fields + redaction safeguards): sources/github/redwood/pr-18521-add-structured-logging-fields-for-private-troubleshooting.json
- Export support work: ENG-19150 (Splunk HEC), ENG-19151 (Elastic/OpenSearch), ENG-19149 (collector defaults)

Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Confirm component-level logging does not emit payload text or secrets at INFO/WARN/ERROR under default config.
- [ ] Verify denylist redaction is applied in collector config templates shipped with the minimum pack.
- [ ] Verify Helm values do not render secrets into ConfigMaps (must be Secrets) and that logs do not print rendered configs on startup.
- [ ] Retention defaults documented and included in values (with clear customer override guidance).
- [ ] Add a lightweight automated “log scrub” test to smoke test harness (fail build on matched secret patterns).
- [ ] Sign-off from Security Engineering (Marcus Lin) + Compliance/Product Security (Naomi Feldman / Kevin Osei as needed).

Open questions
- Do we support OTLP log export in v1 minimum pack, or only document it as “experimental” (per prior debate)? If OTLP logs are supported, ensure metadata filtering aligns with the policy above.
- Should we ship client_ip logging toggle at all in v1, or defer to premium/compliance pack?

Proposed next steps
- Security review meeting: review current log samples from staging private cluster; validate against policy.
- Patch any violations upstream (middleware error logging, request logging interceptors).
- Update docs with explicit do/don’t list for customers and a short incident response note: “How to capture payloads safely if absolutely required.”

title: Security review: audit events + RBAC for Dedicated burst config/admin actions
preview:
Background
We are introducing Dedicated “burst capacity” (shared Redwood-managed burst pools). We must ensure all admin/config actions that affect burst behavior are auditable, permissions are least-privilege, and we have security review evidence ready for GA and for regulated customers (e.g., finance/security questionnaires).

Scope of this ticket
1) Audit logging coverage
- Confirm we emit audit events for all burst-related control plane changes and admin overrides.
- Ensure events contain sufficient context to answer: who did what, to which tenant, what changed, when, from where, and via which mechanism (Console/API/support tooling).
- Ensure sensitive data is not logged (no customer prompts, no request payloads, no secrets).

2) RBAC / authorization
- Validate who is allowed to:
  a) enable/disable burst for a tenant
  b) change burst limits (max burst ratio, tenant cap, cooldown overrides)
  c) change priority tier (if applicable)
  d) toggle region/hardware allowlist gates at the tenant level
  e) use any internal debug endpoints/tools that explain admit/deny decisions
- Confirm Support role permissions align with “view + explain” but not “mutate” defaults.

3) Evidence pack
- Produce a review checklist + links (PRs, unit/integration tests, sample audit log entries, screenshots/queries) that can be attached to the GA readiness checklist and referenced by Sales/CS for security reviews.

Audit events: expected minimum set (to validate against implementation)
Tenant-config level
- dedicated.burst.enabled.changed
- dedicated.burst.limits.changed (includes max_burst_ratio, tenant_burst_cap, cooldown_secs)
- dedicated.burst.priority_tier.changed
- dedicated.burst.region_hardware_allowlist.changed

Operational/admin overrides (time-bounded)
- dedicated.burst.override.applied (e.g., temporary cap reduction during incident mitigation)
- dedicated.burst.override.removed/expired

Debug/inspection access (security monitoring)
- dedicated.burst.debug_endpoint.accessed (internal-only; includes actor + tenant + request_id; excludes prompts/payloads)

Event fields (required)
- actor: principal_id, principal_type (user/service), role, org/team if available
- target: tenant_id, tenant_slug (if safe), region, hardware_class (if applicable)
- action: event_name, outcome (success/failure), reason (if failure)
- change summary: before/after values for changed fields (bounded/normalized)
- request context: request_id/trace_id, source (console/api/support-tool), ip/device if available
- time: timestamp (UTC), environment (prod/staging)

Explicit non-goals
- This ticket does not design customer-facing audit log export; it only ensures the platform emits correct internal audit events and that we can provide evidence for security/compliance.

Acceptance criteria
- Audit event taxonomy is aligned between Confluence spec and implemented code paths (no missing write paths).
- For each burst-related mutation endpoint in the control plane, there is:
  - an authorization check
  - a corresponding audit event on success
  - a corresponding audit event on denied/failed attempts (at least for permission-denied and validation failures)
- Internal debug endpoint/tooling is gated behind an explicit permission and logs access.
- Provide “evidence pack” notes in this Linear ticket (links + sample events + how to query) that Security/Compliance can re-use.

Testing / validation plan
- Create/confirm automated tests (unit or integration) asserting:
  - audit events emitted on enable/disable and limit changes
  - audit events emitted on override apply/remove
  - audit events emitted when request is denied for RBAC
  - redaction rules: payloads not logged
- Manual validation in staging:
  - Use a test tenant; execute each mutation via API/Console; verify events in audit log sink with correct actor/tenant/time.

Risks to call out
- Missing audit events on “bulk update” paths or migration jobs.
- Service-to-service actors: ensure the initiating user is preserved (actor attribution) when requests traverse Console -> control plane -> policy engine.
- Overly broad Support permissions or lack of access logging on debug endpoints.

Owners / reviewers
- Assignee: Marcus Lin (Security Eng)
- Eng partners: Benji Okafor (Control plane), Jordan Lee (Policy engine), Chloe Martin (Console backend)
- Compliance/product review: Naomi Feldman

title: Security review + threat model for SIEM audit-log exporter (secrets, egress/SSRF, leakage, encryption/logging)
preview:
Goal: complete Security Engineering threat model + sign-off for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (hosted + dedicated). Scope is exporter service + destination adapters (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, S3), control-plane config surface, secret handling, and operational surfaces.

This ticket is the gating security review for beta enablement (tenant allowlist). It should produce: (1) a concrete threat model write-up for audit export, (2) actionable mitigations tracked as follow-up issues/PRs, and (3) explicit sign-off criteria for Security + SRE.

Primary areas to review:
- Secrets handling: destination credentials (Splunk HEC token, Datadog API key, AWS role/KMS key IDs), storage, access controls, rotation, redaction in logs.
- SSRF / egress controls: prevent arbitrary outbound requests; destination validation; allowlist strategy; DNS / IP / redirect handling; private IP ranges.
- Data leakage: tenant isolation, cross-tenant config sync, queue separation, DLQ and replay tooling safety, metrics cardinality/labels, structured logs.
- Encryption: in transit to destinations, at rest for checkpoints/DLQ/state, KMS usage, and customer-provided keys where applicable.
- Logging: ensure exporter logs do not contain audit payloads/PII; ensure debug logs are safe; ensure audit event schema v2 meets redaction policy.

References:
- Confluence: Audit event schema v2 + SIEM mapping: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Confluence: PII redaction policy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/pii-redaction-and-sensitive-fields-policy-for-audit-events.json
- ADR: Delivery semantics + idempotency: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency.json
- Exporter architecture: sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/audit-exporter-service-architecture.json
- Slack thread (security review coordination): sources/slack/eng-security/1737395528.json

title: Privacy review: onboarding checklist events are PII-free + retention/compliance aligned
preview:
Context
We are shipping onboarding/progress tracking via a standardized event schema emitted primarily server-side (gateway/control plane) to drive the Console checklist and TTFS/TTFP KPIs.

This ticket is the privacy/security validation pass to ensure:
- Event payloads are PII-free (and do not accidentally capture customer prompts, outputs, headers, or identifiers like emails).
- Retention + residency are aligned with Redwood’s compliance posture and the existing data residency/retention guidance for onboarding telemetry.
- Guardrails exist in code and schema to prevent future regressions.

In scope
- Review onboarding event schema v1 fields + examples used by gateway/control plane + OpenAI-compat layer.
- Validate current emission points and payload construction do not contain PII.
- Confirm retention policy for raw events vs derived checklist state vs aggregated KPIs.
- Confirm processing location / regional storage constraints for EU tenants.
- Confirm access controls (who can query raw events; how Support uses checklist state).

Out of scope
- Full DPIA for all telemetry across product (focus is onboarding/checklist events only).
- Private deployment customer-controlled telemetry pipeline (MVP is Hosted API cohorts).

Risk summary (why this matters)
- Onboarding events fire early in the lifecycle and could include user-controlled fields if we are not strict (e.g., error_message, user_agent, request metadata).
- Support workflows and admin override tooling may expose audit trails; must avoid storing sensitive details in “reason” fields.

Findings (review results)
1) Allowed identifiers
- OK: internal tenant identifiers (tenant_id / org_id), internal project/workspace id, environment (prod/sandbox), region, cohort id.
- OK with constraints: api_key_id as an internal UUID (NOT the key string). If callers currently only have a key fingerprint, that is acceptable as long as it is irreversible and length-capped.
- NOT allowed: customer emails, end-user ids, company name, billing contact, IP addresses, raw request headers, prompt/response text, function/tool arguments, or any free-form user-provided metadata.

2) High-risk fields identified and mitigation
- error_message: must NOT be included as a free-form string due to potential leakage of prompt fragments or user identifiers. Replace with enumerated error_code (e.g., AUTH_INVALID_KEY, RATE_LIMITED, UPSTREAM_TIMEOUT) and optional error_class.
- user_agent: permitted for funnel attribution only if normalized/truncated and not stored verbatim. Action: store sdk_name/sdk_version explicitly; store a normalized ua_family and cap length to 128 chars; drop anything beyond allowlist patterns.
- endpoint/path: must be stored as a canonical route key (e.g., /v1/chat.completions) and NOT full URL including query parameters.
- request_id: OK; must be Redwood-generated correlation id (not customer-provided) and treated as operational metadata.

3) Payload examples sampled
Reviewed sample payloads from:
- API gateway emission (first 2xx, first streaming established, first 429)
- Control plane (API key created, quota/budget configured)
- OpenAI-compat layer UA tagging

Result: no prompt/response content observed in sample payloads. The one exception found during review was an early implementation path that attached error_message from upstream as-is for a small set of 5xx/timeout cases.

Decision: block free-form error_message and ship with error_code only.

4) Retention + access control alignment
Proposed/confirmed retention tiers (matches onboarding telemetry expectations):
- Raw onboarding events dataset: 30 days retention (operational debugging window). Access limited to Telemetry/Infra on-call and security-approved analysts.
- Derived checklist progress state (per-tenant step state): retained while feature is enabled + 13 months for audit/CS context; contains only step status + timestamps (no request metadata).
- Aggregated KPIs (TTFS/TTFP, funnel metrics): 13 months retention for trend analysis; no per-request identifiers.

Data residency
- EU tenants: raw events and derived datasets must be stored/processed within EU analytics boundary when available; otherwise, onboarding checklist exposure remains gated by cohort rules (feature flag) until EU pipeline is verified.

5) Support/admin override considerations
- Admin override “reason” field must be constrained to an allowlist (e.g., CUSTOMER_REPORTED_BUG, BACKFILL_CORRECTION, INTERNAL_TEST) plus optional internal-only note capped to 256 chars and explicitly disallowing customer-provided text.
- Support-facing UI should display step completion info only (timestamps, step ids) and must not expose raw event payloads.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Schema updated to remove/ban free-form error_message; replace with error_code + error_class.
- [x] Schema explicitly documents “PII disallowed” and contains a short allowlist/denylist section.
- [x] Ingestion enforces max lengths + drops unknown fields (no pass-through JSON blobs).
- [x] Gateway/control plane emission updated to conform (no upstream error strings).
- [x] Retention documented (raw 30d, derived 13mo, aggregates 13mo) and confirmed with Security.
- [x] EU residency constraint documented + tied to rollout gating.
- [x] Support/admin override audit trail avoids sensitive notes (allowlist + cap).

Implementation notes / guardrails
- Add validation at ingestion: reject events with disallowed keys; cap all string fields; enforce enums.
- Add a CI check for schema changes: "no new string fields without explicit classification" (operational vs identifier vs user-controlled) and a reviewer requirement for Security.
- Add a sampling job that periodically scans raw events for suspicious patterns (emails, credit card regex, long base64 blobs) and alerts #security-and-compliance.

Rollout implications
- Checklist feature flag should remain off for EU cohorts until EU analytics storage is confirmed (separate tracking ticket if needed).
- Backfill job must write derived/aggregate metrics only; should not attempt to reconstruct request-level event payloads.

Deliverables / artifacts
- Updated schema + docs.
- Sign-off note from Security.
- Follow-up tasks created for any non-blocking improvements.

title: Emit audit log events for Hosted rollout actions (create/start/step/pause/approve/rollback) with actor + diffs
preview:
We need GA-quality audit logging coverage for Hosted canary rollouts. Emit customer-visible, exportable audit log events for rollout create/start/step-change/pause/resume/approve/rollback/cancel actions. Events must include: tenant, environment, route identifiers, rollout_id, previous/new state, actor identity (user/service), request correlation IDs, and a safe diff of relevant config fields. Also emit events for system-initiated actions (e.g., auto-pause/auto-rollback on SLO breach) with an explicit system actor + reason metadata.

This is a GA exit requirement for regulated customers (change control + investigations) and is explicitly requested in security reviews.

title: Audit log events for route experiment lifecycle (create/update/start/stop) + export/retention verification
preview:
## Context
Route-level A/B testing introduces customer-visible configuration that changes request routing behavior. For enterprise customers (esp. regulated) we need complete auditability for experiment lifecycle actions (create/update/start/stop), including who changed what, when, from where, and with what resulting state.

This ticket implements and validates audit events for:
- experiment.create
- experiment.update
- experiment.start
- experiment.stop

…and verifies:
- retention meets compliance expectations
- export format includes required fields for SIEM/Splunk/S3 pipelines
- redaction rules are correct (no prompts, no secrets)

Primary references:
- Confluence: security-and-compliance/audit-logging/experiment-config-audit-events.json
- GA requirements: PM-482

## Requirements / Event model
### Event names
Use the existing Redwood audit event conventions:
- resource_type: "experiment"
- action: "created" | "updated" | "started" | "stopped"
- event_name: "experiment.created" etc.

### Required top-level fields (exported)
For each event, ensure these fields are populated and included in audit export:
- event_id (UUID)
- event_time (RFC3339)
- actor_type ("user" | "service")
- actor_id
- actor_email (if actor_type=user)
- actor_role (best-effort; RBAC role at time of action)
- organization_id (tenant)
- environment ("prod" | "staging" etc.)
- source ("console" | "api" | "internal")
- request_id / trace_id (for correlation)
- ip_address (if available)
- user_agent (if available)

### Experiment-specific required fields
Include in event payload (and export):
- experiment_id
- experiment_name
- route_id (normalized route identifier; template form)
- route_path_template (if available)
- state_before / state_after (e.g., DRAFT -> RUNNING)
- config_version_before / config_version_after

### Change diff for update events
For experiment.updated, include a compact, human-readable diff summary string plus a machine-friendly list (when supported by the exporter).

Constraints:
- Must not include full prompt bodies, model outputs, or customer request payloads.
- Must not include auth headers, API keys, cookies.

Diff summary examples:
- "allocation: control=0.5->0.9, treatment=0.5->0.1"
- "guardrails.p99_ms: 1200->1000"
- "eligibility: added header rule X-Plan==enterprise"

### Start/stop semantics
- experiment.started should fire when state transitions to RUNNING and the router/policy engine config publish is accepted.
- experiment.stopped should fire when state transitions out of RUNNING (STOPPED / ENDED).
- If stop has a drain period, include stop_mode ("immediate" | "drain") and drain_deadline if applicable.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Console/API mutations for create/update/start/stop emit audit events exactly once per successful state transition.
- [x] Events include required actor + tenant + experiment identifiers.
- [x] experiment.updated includes diff summary string and references config_version_before/after.
- [x] Events are queryable in internal audit UI and in audit export dataset.
- [x] Verified retention: audit events retained >= 400 days in prod (per enterprise compliance baseline).
- [x] Verified export format: fields present and stable; downstream parsers (SIEM) can key on event_name, organization_id, actor_id, experiment_id.
- [x] Redaction validated: no request content, no secrets.

## Implementation notes
- Hook points are in the experiment service / Console backend state machine.
- Emit after DB commit to avoid orphaned events for failed transactions.
- For update events, diff is computed from persisted config (before) vs proposed config (after validation).
- Ensure service-to-service actions (e.g., auto-stop/guardrail triggers) are actor_type=service and include service name (e.g., "guardrail-evaluator") in actor_id.

## Test plan
- Unit tests:
  - create emits experiment.created with state_after=DRAFT and config_version_after=1
  - start emits experiment.started with state_before=DRAFT, state_after=RUNNING
  - stop emits experiment.stopped with expected stop_mode and state_after
  - update emits experiment.updated with non-empty diff_summary and version bump
- Integration tests:
  - RBAC-gated user performs action in Console -> audit event contains actor_email + request_id
  - Guardrail soft-stop -> actor_type=service and event emitted
- Redaction tests:
  - Ensure no headers/payload fields are serialized into audit payload

## Rollout / Backwards compatibility
- Export schema is additive only; no field removals.
- For any missing actor fields (e.g., internal batch operations), set actor_email="" and ensure actor_id is set.

## Links
- Confluence requirements: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/experiment-config-audit-events.json
- Related PRs:
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-28539-add-audit-log-events-for-experiment-lifecycle.json
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-28719-add-audit-export-fields-for-experiment-events.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-9024 (Console APIs)
  - ENG-9029 (RBAC permissions)

## Notes / Open questions (resolved)
- Q: Do we need experiment.delete for GA?
  - A: Not for GA cut; lifecycle includes create/update/start/stop. Delete is admin-only and will be tracked in a follow-up ticket if product enables it in UI.
- Q: Retention requirement?
  - A: Align with enterprise baseline: 400 days in prod audit store; export pipeline mirrors retention in customer-owned sink.

## Outcome
Shipped audit events for create/update/start/stop for route experiments and verified retention + export fields. Security review completed with no P0 findings; redaction rules validated against sample payloads from staging.
